Shift4 claims crypto momentum as it partners with Lydian to support Tether (USDT) payment acceptance
Source: Retail Technology Innovation Hub    Published: 2026-05-12 04:58:01

Shift4, a specialist in integrated payments and commerce technology, and Lydian, a crypto and stablecoin payment platform, have announced a partnership to expand upon Shift4's Pay with Crypto solution, enabling the latter's merchants to accept all digital assets, in addition to Tether (USDT), with settlement in local currency. The Pay with Crypto product allows customers to pay with any major wallet, just as they would with a credit card. Merchants receive funds directly in their local currency without holding or touching digital assets. The Lydian partnership adds stablecoin, Tether, to the list of crypto payment options supported on the platform, with no new workflows or crypto expertise needed from merchants. “We built our Pay with Crypto solution because it is becoming a mainstream payment method. This seamless, secure way to accept Tether expands on our successful programme without added complexity or risk. We're giving merchants the tools to capture a fast-growing base of stablecoin paying customers,” says Taylor Lauber, CEO at Shift4. Pay with Crypto is now live across thousands of merchants in the United States. “Lydian was designed to bridge the gap between traditional money and digital assets by making crypto function exactly like traditional payments at checkout. Our mission is to bring digital assets, like USDT, into everyday commerce, allowing merchants to offer new ways to pay without introducing new risks or operational complexity,” says Carl Grimstad, CEO at Lydian. 2026 RTIH Innovation Awards Payments will be a key focus area at the 2026 RTIH Innovation Awards. The awards are now open for entries and celebrate global retail technology innovation in a fast moving omnichannel world. Our winners will be revealed at the 2026 RTIH Innovation Awards Ceremony, taking place at The HAC in Central London on Wednesday, 4th November. Check out our 2025 winners here. Our 2025 hall of fame entrants were revealed during a sold out event which took place at The HAC on 16th October and consisted of a drinks reception, three course meal, and awards ceremony presided over by award winning comedian, actress and writer Tiff Stevenson. In his welcome speech, Scott Thompson, Founder and Editor, RTIH, said: “This is the awards' fifth year as a physical event. We started off with just 30 people at the South Place Hotel not far from here, then moved to London Bridge Hotel, then The Barbican, and last year RIBA's HQ in the West End.” “But I'm conscious of the fact that, to quote the legend that is Taylor Swift, You're only as hot as your last hit, baby. So, this year we've moved to our biggest venue yet, and also pulled in our largest number of entries to date and broken attendance records.” He added: “This year's submissions have without doubt been our best yet. To quote one of the judges: The examples of innovative developments across both traditional and digital retail spaces were truly remarkable.” Congratulations to our winners, and a big thank you to our sponsors, judging panel, the legend that is Tiff Stevenson, and all those who attended our 2025 gathering. XRP, Bitcoin and Ethereum as Institutional Collateral Is the Next Step Says Ripple Prime CEO
Source: TradingView    Published: 2026-05-12 04:54:00

Ripple Prime CEO Mike Higgins says cryptocurrencies like XRP, Bitcoin, Ethereum, and Solana could soon play a much larger role in institutional finance through cross-margining and collateral systems. Speaking about the future of tokenized finance, Higgins explained that institutions may eventually use digital assets, stablecoins, and tokenized money market funds as collateral for settlement and margin requirements, rather than relying solely on dollars or U.S. Treasuries. “Bitcoin, Ethereum, XRP, and Solana tokenizing anything of value as collateral for margin and settlement is the next step,” Higgins said. XRP Utility Expands Beyond Payments According to Higgins, the next phase of crypto adoption is not just about trading or payments but about using blockchain assets as high-grade collateral across financial markets. He explained that cross-margining allows institutions to use assets efficiently across multiple products without first liquidating positions. This could improve capital efficiency while increasing the real-world utility of blockchain networks like the XRP Ledger. Higgins also pointed out that major crypto assets are increasingly being treated similarly to products listed on regulated exchanges, especially as institutional infrastructure continues improving. “We're still early on in the space,” he said, while noting that trading opportunities across crypto spot markets, futures, ETFs, and perpetual swaps remain significant. Ripple's Hidden Road Acquisition Plays Key Role Higgins linked this vision directly to Ripple's acquisition of Hidden Road, which now operates as Ripple Prime. The platform focuses heavily on cross-margining between crypto spot markets, ETFs, futures, and options markets. According to Higgins, institutions are already building strategies involving spot Bitcoin, Bitcoin ETFs, and CME futures contracts. However, he believes stronger infrastructure is still needed to support smoother cross-market settlement and collateral management. The comments highlight Ripple's broader push into institutional finance as the company continues expanding beyond payments and deeper into tokenization, trading infrastructure, and digital asset settlement services. XRP and XLM outlook: Momentum indicators signal growing strength as bulls eye breakout
Source: FXStreet    Published: 2026-05-12 04:10:36

Rates & Charts News Analysis Economic Calendar Cryptos Education Brokers Ripple (XRP) and Stellar (XLM) prices trade cautiously at the time of writing on Tuesday, with improving momentum indicators and supportive on-chain metrics keeping hopes alive for an upside breakout for both altcoins. XRP is testing the upper boundary of its descending parallel channel, where a break above suggests a fresh rally. Meanwhile, XLM continues to consolidate between its key 50-day and 100-day Exponential Moving Averages (EMAs), signaling indecision ahead of a directional move. CryptoQuant summary data suggests a neutral to slightly bullish outlook for XRP and XLM. XRP's spot markets show cooling conditions and buy-side dominance, with mostly neutral conditions across other metrics, suggesting a potential upside move. Meanwhile, for XLM, the data shows buy-side dominance with largely neutral conditions across other metrics, suggesting mild bullish signs. On the derivatives side, Ripple and Stellar data show a positive outlook. The funding rates data for both altcoins support improving sentiment. CoinGlass' OI-Weighted Funding Rate data for XRP and XLM flipped positive on Friday, reading 0.0048% and 0.0030%, respectively, on Tuesday. This positive rate indicates that longs are paying shorts and projecting a bullish sentiment. XRP is trading at $1.47 on Tuesday and holds above the 50-day EMA around $1.41, but remains capped by the upper boundary of the prevailing downward parallel channel near $1.48 and the 100-day EMA at $1.49, keeping the broader tone neutral despite the latest rebound. The Relative Strength Index (RSI) on the daily chart hovers in the low 60s. At the same time, the Moving Average Convergence Divergence (MACD) remains above the zero line, suggesting improving bullish momentum, yet remains constrained by the nearby cluster of overhead EMAs and channel resistance. On the downside, immediate support emerges at the 50-day EMA around $1.41, with the next notable floor at the horizontal level of $1.30, ahead of the lower boundary of the broader descending channel near $0.66. On the topside, initial resistance is seen at the channel top around $1.48, followed by the 100-day EMA at $1.49; a sustained break above these levels would expose the 200-day EMA near $1.71 and, beyond that, the major horizontal barrier around $1.90. Stellar price is trading at $0.167 on Tuesday, keeping a capped bias as price holds above the 50-day EMA around $0.165 but remains firmly below the 100-day EMA at $0.174 and the 200-day EMA near $0.204, as well as broader descending trendline resistance. The RSI on the daily chart hovers just above the midline near 54, and the MACD line edges slightly above zero, hinting at mild bullish momentum that has yet to overcome the dominant overhead structure. On the topside, initial resistance is seen at the 100-day EMA around $0.174, with the 23.6% Fibonacci retracement of the broader downswing aligning near $0.201 just ahead of the 200-day EMA at about $0.204 to form a dense supply zone. In contrast, higher up, the 38.2% Fibonacci retracement stands near $0.241 and deeper levels at $0.274 and $0.307 cap the medium-term recovery path. On the downside, immediate support is provided by the 50-day EMA at $0.165, with a more important structural floor emerging at the 0.0% Fibonacci anchor near $0.136, where a break would likely reopen the broader bearish phase despite the current stabilization in momentum indicators. (The technical analysis of this story was written with the help of an AI tool.) Author Manish Chhetri FXStreet Manish Chhetri is a crypto specialist with over four years of experience in the cryptocurrency industry. Vishal Dixit Manish Chhetri Vishal Dixit Manish Chhetri Michael Ebiekutan Manish Chhetri XRP slides from the critical $1.50 supply zone, tests the $1.45 support level on Monday. Fresh tensions between the US and Iran emerge as Trump dismisses the latest Iranian proposal to end the war. Bitcoin slides toward $80,000 as geopolitical tensions weigh on sentiment after Trump rejected Iran's latest proposal to end the war. Ethereum slips below the 100-day EMA amid a weakening technical outlook and mild ETF inflows last week. Bitcoin trades lower on Monday, below $81,000 as of writing, after surging over 4.5% the previous week. Institutional demand supported BTC's bullish price action, with spot Exchange Traded Funds (ETFs) recording their sixth consecutive week of inflows. Pi Network edges lower on Monday, risking a steeper correction below $0.1700 amid the broader crypto market shake-off. PiScan, the on-chain analytics platform for Pi Network, is down amid the Mainnet upgrade to protocol v23, which is pressuring retail strength. Bitcoin (BTC) is wrapping up 2025 as one of its most eventful years, defined by unprecedented institutional participation, major regulatory developments, and extreme price volatility. Bitcoin (BTC) pauses its recent rally, trading below $80,000 on Friday with modest weekly gains, as it struggles to break above a key overhead supply zone. Korea Should Adopt Risk-Based Stablecoin Rules Instead of Blanket Regulation
Source: bloomingbit    Published: 2026-05-12 02:24:27

Summary Forecast Trend Report by Period South Korea should adopt a differentiated regulatory framework for stablecoins based on the risk profile of each digital asset, rather than imposing uniform rules across the board, speakers at a seminar in Seoul said. They added that Korea should design a system suited to its own market by studying the results and side effects seen in major jurisdictions that have already rolled out digital-asset regulation. Joshua Townson, global head of regulatory affairs at DCGG, or Digital Currency Governance Group, made the remarks at the “Global Stablecoin Trends and Opportunities for Korea's Digital Economy” seminar held on May 12 at the National Assembly Members' Office Building in Yeouido, Seoul. Major economies are accelerating efforts to build stablecoin regulatory frameworks, but results have diverged depending on the regulatory approach taken in each market, he said. Townson said the European Union had produced disappointing results under the Markets in Crypto-Assets regulation, or MiCA, because of its overly stringent approach. MiCA applies strict, uniform rules to stablecoins. As a result, some companies and liquidity have left the market, he said. As an example Korea could look to, Townson cited the U.K.'s digital-asset legislation. The U.K. has adopted an approach that varies regulatory intensity depending on risk levels and use cases, he said. Given Korea's high level of digital-asset adoption, the country has an opportunity to build a world-class regulatory framework by learning from the strengths and weaknesses of overseas regimes. Kim Tae-rim, managing partner at AXIS Law, said uniform regulation modeled on the EU's MiCA framework could encourage regulatory arbitrage and drive participants out of the market. Korea should use a risk-proportionate model similar to the U.K.'s as a reference for its own regulatory system, applying different rules according to the risk level of each digital asset, he said. He added that major countries are moving toward bringing markets into the regulatory system through safeguards rather than blocking them outright. Korea should also focus on building a structure that allows the market to be managed within the institutional framework rather than trying to hold back its development, he said. Uk Jin Contact Us help@bloomingbit.io

Circle Internet Group Gains 16% as Arc Blockchain Raise Draws Blackrock and Apollo
Source: Bitcoin News    Published: 2026-05-12 01:13:15

While American bankers, lawmakers, stablecoin issuers, and crypto exchanges trade barbs, Circle investors appear entirely unbothered. Circle Internet Group (NYSE: CRCL) closed roughly 16% higher on Monday at Wall Street's close, after the stablecoin issuer announced a $222 million pre-launch token sale for its new layer-one (L1) blockchain project, Arc, backed by Blackrock, A16z, Apollo, and Ark Invest. Shares of CRCL traded between $129 and $134 on heavy volume, touching intraday highs near 19% before settling into the close. The session pushed Circle's 2026 year-to-date gain to approximately 68%. The move came on the same day the company reported first-quarter 2026 earnings, which delivered a mixed picture on revenue but showed record adoption numbers for USDC, its dollar-pegged stablecoin. Circle reported total revenue and reserve income of $694 million for Q1, up 20% year-over-year but short of some Street estimates near $715 million. Net income from continuing operations came in at $55 million, down 15% from the prior-year period, pressured by higher post-IPO stock-based compensation and growth spending. Adjusted EBITDA climbed roughly 24%. The numbers investors focused on were the USDC metrics. USDC in circulation reached $77.0 billion at quarter-end, a 28% increase year-over-year. Onchain transaction volume processed through USDC hit $21.5 trillion for the quarter, up 263% from Q1 2025. That figure spans payments, decentralized finance (DeFi), and tokenized assets, and it showed the network handling real transaction load well beyond what the revenue line reflected. CEO Jeremy Allaire addressed analysts on a conference call at 8:00 a.m. ET, describing the quarter as part of a broad platform shift in the financial industry. He emphasized that USDC's growth operates independently of crypto price cycles, citing stablecoin infrastructure scaling and positioning in payments and tokenization as the core story. The earnings release ran alongside an announcement that Circle raised $222 million in a pre-launch token sale for Arc, valuing the project at a $3 billion fully diluted valuation. Arc is an L1 blockchain designed for payments, tokenization, and what Circle calls an “Agent Stack” for AI and blockchain convergence. The raise made Circle the first public company to execute a pre-launch token sale. Investors in the Arc raise include A16z, Blackrock, Apollo, Ark Invest, and Intercontinental Exchange (ICE). The involvement of those names shifted the conversation from a quarterly earnings beat-or-miss to a longer-term infrastructure play. Traders cited the Arc announcement as the primary catalyst for the afternoon acceleration, with the stock gapping up pre-market, dipping on the mixed financials, then climbing sharply as the raise details circulated. Circle investors do not appear worried about the ongoing dispute between traditional bankers and stablecoin issuers and exchanges over the CLARITY Act, the bipartisan stablecoin regulatory framework advancing through Congress in early May. Retail sentiment on platforms like Stocktwits registered as extremely bullish on the session, with CRCL frequently mentioned alongside other crypto-linked names. Other crypto equities also moved higher on Monday as well. Coinbase Global (Nasdaq: COIN) rose 7.68% intraday, trading around $216.60 with strong volume. Strategy (Nasdaq: MSTR) gained roughly 4.5%, trading over $195. Bitgo (NYSE: BTGO), which went public in January 2026, climbed approximately 3.8%, trading around $12.97 per share. Broader U.S. equity markets closed in positive territory. The Dow Jones Industrial Average added 95.31 points to finish at 49,704.47. The S&P 500 gained 13.91 points to close at 7,412.84, an all-time high. The Nasdaq Composite climbed 27.05 points to 26,274.12, and the NYSE Composite added 23.38 points to close at 22,965.53. Analysts watching CRCL noted that short-term volatility remains elevated for crypto-adjacent names, as gap-and-fill patterns are common. Several have flagged $150 as an upside target if USDC adoption trends hold and the Arc project gains traction in the months ahead. If metrics favor Solana, why does institutional capital choose Ethereum?
Source: AMBCrypto    Published: 2026-05-12 00:00:44

Read the best crypto stories of the day in less than 5 minutes. Join to get it daily in your inbox. Thanks for subscribing to Unhashed 🚀 Read the best crypto stories of the day in less than 5 minutes. Join to get it daily in your inbox. Thanks for subscribing to Unhashed 🚀 What compels an institutional heavyweight to launch a product on a particular blockchain? At a fundamental level, institutions tend to evaluate core infrastructure metrics such as transaction speed, execution costs, and settlement finality when choosing a Layer-1 network. The logic is simple: Faster confirmation times and lower fees allow a blockchain to handle higher transaction throughput, which directly supports a smoother user experience. Viewed through this lens, Solana [SOL] stands out. As the chart below shows, Solana processed 696 million transactions over the past week. Every other chain combined processed about 593 million. One network alone generated more activity than the rest of crypto, accounting for 54% of total on-chain transactions. As discussed earlier, high transaction volume is often a direct reflection of a chain's underlying metrics. To put this into perspective, comparing Solana with Ethereum [ETH] helps highlight the gap. According to Chainspect data, Solana recently achieved transaction fees that are nearly 100% lower than Ethereum's. Meanwhile, the first successful Alpenglow upgrade cut finalization times from roughly 12.8 seconds to around 100-150 milliseconds, pushing Solana closer to near-instant settlement speeds. Given this setup, one would reasonably expect large institutional players to lean toward Solana for product launches. Yet BlackRock opted for Ethereum for its money market fund launch instead, raising a key question: Does institutional capital still hesitate to price Solana as fundamentally undervalued? One key takeaway from BlackRock's latest move is how important trust is for institutions. For context, institutional giant BlackRock is launching two tokenized money market funds. One of the funds is tied to a $6.1 billion Treasury liquidity vehicle and is designed primarily for stablecoin holders rather than bank account users. The move highlights a broader shift toward integrating TradFi with DeFi, enabling capital to move more efficiently on-chain. The key takeaway? BlackRock chose Ethereum for the launch. This creates a clear divergence. From an on-chain efficiency standpoint, Solana leads with stronger infrastructure. Naturally, the question follows: Why did BlackRock still choose Ethereum? The answer largely comes down to liquidity. As the chart below shows, despite the recent market crunch, Ethereum still dominates total value locked (TVL), holding over 50% of on-chain liquidity compared to Solana's 6.7%. For institutions, deeper liquidity means greater confidence when launching large financial products. And as discussed earlier, trust in an ecosystem plays a major role in institutional decision-making. In this context, Solana's perceived undervaluation may not be about a weaker technical setup. Instead, it's about institutions still assigning a premium to liquidity depth over pure performance advantages. Ritika Gupta is a coin-based journalist at AMBCrypto who focuses on how economic and political trends impact cryptocurrencies. A social sciences graduate from Gargi College, she reports on AI, DeFi, Web3, and blockchain, using her hands-on experience to turn complex crypto developments into clear, practical insights for readers. SCIENCE

MEK interactions tune RAF kinase sensitivity to conformation-selective inhibition
Source: Nature    Published: 2026-05-12 09:43:24

Thank you for visiting nature.com. You are using a browser version with limited support for CSS. To obtain the best experience, we recommend you use a more up to date browser (or turn off compatibility mode in Internet Explorer). In the meantime, to ensure continued support, we are displaying the site without styles and JavaScript. Advertisement Nature Chemical Biology (2026) Cite this article RAF kinases are key effectors in the RAS–RAF–MEK–ERK signaling pathway, making them important targets for the development of cancer therapeutics. Here we investigate the variable potency of DFG-out-stabilizing RAF inhibitors in mutant KRAS-expressing cell lines. We demonstrate that inhibitor potency correlates with basal RAF activity, with more active RAF being more sensitive to inhibition. We further show that DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors disrupt high-affinity RAF–MEK interactions, promoting the formation of inhibited RAF dimers. Furthermore, we identify cobimetinib as an MEK inhibitor that uniquely sensitizes RAF kinases to DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors by disrupting autoinhibited RAF–MEK complexes. Building on this insight, we developed cobimetinib analogs with enhanced sensitization properties. Together, our findings provide a mechanistic framework for understanding the cellular determinants of DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitor sensitivity and offer strategies for optimizing synergistic RAF–MEK inhibitor combinations. The RAF family protein kinases, consisting of A-RAF, B-RAF and C-RAF (RAF1) in humans, are major downstream effectors of activated RAS1. In the absence of active RAS, wild-type (WT) RAF kinases are maintained in an autoinhibited monomeric state within a complex with inactive MEK and two 14-3-3 proteins2,3,4. Activation of RAS recruits RAF homodimers and heterodimers to the plasma membrane, where they phosphorylate the activation loop of MEK (MEK1/2)5. The resulting activation-loop-phosphorylated MEK1/2 then activates its sole substrate, ERK1/2, through activation loop phosphorylation. ERK1/2, in turn, phosphorylates multiple substrates to modulate signaling and transcriptional programs that drive diverse cellular processes including differentiation, proliferation and survival6,7. The prevalence of somatic mutations in B-RAF and the upstream RAS GTPases that promote oncogenic signaling has motivated the development of RAF kinase inhibitors8,9. Several first-generation, ATP-competitive RAF inhibitors have been approved as single agents or as combination therapies with MEK inhibitors by the US Food and Drug Administration10. These inhibitors stabilize a conformation of RAF's ATP-binding site where a helix in the N-terminal lobe of the kinase domain—called the αC helix—is outwardly displaced from its active conformation. While effective against B-RAF V600 mutants, these αC helix-out-stabilizing inhibitors paradoxically activate downstream MEK and ERK (Fig. 1a) across a wide concentration range in cells expressing non-V600 B-RAF mutants or mutant RAS11,12,13,14. This paradoxical activation arises because αC helix-out-stabilizing inhibitors promote RAF dimerization with negative cooperativity between protomer ATP-binding sites, resulting in catalytically active, single-inhibitor-occupied dimers12,13. a, Illustration showing how RAF inhibitors that stabilize the αC helix-out or DFG-out conformations affect downstream MEK and ERK activation at different concentrations. αC helix-out-stabilizing inhibitors exhibit ‘paradoxical activation' across a broad concentration range because of negative cooperativity between the ATP-binding sites of inhibitor-promoted dimers. DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors do not demonstrate negative cooperativity between the ATP-binding sites of inhibitor-promoted RAF dimers and second protomer inhibitor occupancy can be determined with both pERK/pMEK IC50 and RAF DC50 values. b, Heat map representing literature pERK IC50 values for various αC helix-out-stabilizing and DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors in cell lines that express WT RAF and mutant KRAS. Boxes in white indicate that no pERK IC50 values were reported. The darkest blue boxes denote a pERK IC50 value > 1 μM. c, Structure of LXH-254. d,e, Representative western blots (d) and pERK IC50 curves (e) from mutant KRAS-expressing cells treated with LXH-254 (4 h) (n = 3, *n = 1 or **n = 2 biological replicates). Data points represent the mean of all biological replicates. f,g, Representative western blots (f) and pMEK IC50 curves (g) from mutant KRAS-expressing cells treated with LXH-254 (4 h). Data points represent the mean of n = 2 biological replicates. h, Correlation plot of LXH-254 pERK and pMEK IC50 values across a panel of KRAS mutant cell lines. Pearson's r value is shown. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals (CIs). pERK and pMEK IC50 values and 95% CIs for e,g are listed in Supplementary Table 1. Source data Inhibitors that stabilize the DFG-out conformation, where the DFG motif in RAF's activation loop is flipped, have been shown to effectively blunt downstream signaling in multiple cellular contexts15,16,17. Unlike αC helix-out-stabilizing inhibitors, DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors potently block kinase activity despite promoting dimer formation, as they lack negative cooperativity between inhibitor-bound protomers (Fig. 1a)18,19,20. Consequently, this class of inhibitors holds promise for treating cancers with dysregulated RAS and RAF activity. Nevertheless, key mechanistic questions remain. The impact of cellular signaling state on the potency of these inhibitors remains incompletely defined, as does the mechanistic basis for observed differences in their ability to block the RAF–MEK–ERK pathway. Furthermore, it is unclear whether the same MEK inhibitors that have been successfully paired with αC helix-out-stabilizing inhibitors in treating mutant B-RAF V600 tumors will show similar effectiveness in combination with DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors. Here, we demonstrate that the potency of DFG-out-stabilizing RAF inhibitors is highly signaling state dependent in cells that express mutant KRAS and WT RAF kinases. We find that, counterintuitively, the more active RAF is within a cell, the easier it is to inhibit, with differences in basal RAF activity accounting for observed variations in potency. By quantifying intracellular target engagement across different RAF signaling complexes, we show that inhibitor potency is determined by the ease with which DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors recruit autoinhibited, monomeric RAF into dimers. A screen for synergistic MEK inhibitor cotreatment strategies revealed that cobimetinib uniquely sensitizes RAF to DFG-out inhibition by disrupting autoinhibited RAF complexes and promoting active dimers. Leveraging this insight, we show that analogs of cobimetinib can be generated that provide enhanced sensitization to DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors. Together, our findings provide a mechanistic framework for understanding the intracellular determinants of DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitor sensitivity and inform the development of synergistic cotreatment strategies. To understand how cellular context influences RAF inhibitor sensitivity, we compiled reported potencies for blocking downstream ERK activation (pERK half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) values) across cell lines expressing WT RAF and mutant KRAS (Fig. 1b)12,21,22,23,24,25,26,27. As expected, αC helix-out-stabilizing inhibitors showed limited potency in these cells, consistent with their propensity to form single-inhibitor-occupied RAF dimers that paradoxically activate downstream signaling. In contrast, inhibitors that stabilize the DFG-out conformation potently blocked ERK activation (Fig. 1b). However, reported pERK IC50 values for the same DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitor ranged from double-digit nanomolar to micromolar, demonstrating marked cell-context-dependent potency. As reported pERK IC50 values of DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors lack systematic comparison across mutant KRAS-expressing cell lines, we conducted our own analysis using LXH-254 (1), a highly selective DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitor (Fig. 1c)8,28. Before performing full dose responses with LXH-254, we conducted a time-course inhibition study in HCT-116 (KRAS-G13D, WT RAF) cells to determine the shortest treatment time that provided maximal decreases in pERK levels. We observed that, following initial transient paradoxical activation, maximal and stable inhibition of downstream signaling was achieved after 4 h of LXH-254 treatment (Extended Data Fig. 1a). We then measured pERK IC50 values after 4 h of LXH-254 treatment across a diverse panel of 11 mutant KRAS-expressing cancer cell lines (Fig. 1d,e). We observed a range of pERK IC50 values, from double-digit nanomolar (MDA-MB-231 and HCT-116 cells) to micromolar (MDA-MB-453) (Supplementary Table 1). A representative panel of six lines covering this full potency range was then used to assess LXH-254's ability to block phosphorylation of RAF's direct substrate MEK (pMEK) (Fig. 1f,g). Consistent with pERK levels accurately reflecting RAF activity, intracellular pMEK and pERK IC50 values were well correlated (Fig. 1h). Lastly, we determined whether the relative potencies of LXH-254 at 4 h were representative of pathway inhibition at later time points by measuring pERK IC50 values in MDA-MB-453 (insensitive), Mia PaCa-2 (moderate sensitivity) and HCT-116 (sensitive) cells after 24 h (Extended Data Fig. 1b). Although pERK IC50 values increased across all three lines, relative potency differences were consistent with those observed at 4 h LXH-254 treatment, indicating that cell-context-dependent sensitivity persists under more prolonged pathway inhibition. We next asked whether LXH-254's variable potency across mutant KRAS-expressing cells is shared by other inhibitors that stabilize the DFG-out conformation. Therefore, we profiled an expanded inhibitor panel (Fig. 2a) including AZ628 (5) and tovorafenib (4), which, along with LXH-254, have been structurally validated to stabilize the DFG-out conformation (Fig. 2b and Extended Data Fig. 1c)19,21, and a newly developed pan-RAF inhibitor LF-268 (2) (Extended Data Fig. 1d), which possesses a DFG-out-stabilizing pharmacophore but has not been structurally characterized. Together, these inhibitors span a range of inhibitory potencies and structurally diverse ATP-competitive scaffolds13,19. Before conducting intracellular pathway inhibition analyses, we performed competitive kinobead-based inhibitor profiling (Fig. 2c, Extended Data Fig. 1e, Supplementary Fig. 1a and Supplementary Table 2)29,30,31 of the inhibitors in our panel and intracellular target engagement (Extended Data Fig. 1f and Supplementary Fig. 2a)32 and phosphokinomic (Extended Data Fig. 1g) analysis of LF-268. Consistent with previously reported profiling data28, we found that LXH-254 was the most selective inhibitor. AZ628, tovorafenib and LF-268 were less selective but showed complementary off-target profiles, with the receptor tyrosine kinase DDR2 as the only off-target kinase potently engaged by all four inhibitors (Fig. 2c and Supplementary Fig. 1a). a, Structures of AZ628, tovorafenib and LF-268. b, Superimposition of the ATP-binding sites of the active (orange; PDB 6XAG) and LXH-254-bound B-RAF (blue; PDB 8F7P) structures. The side chain of F594 in the DFG motif is shown in stick form. c, Venn diagram showing the number of unique and shared off-target kinases significantly competed (>75%) by LXH-254, AZ628, tovorafenib and LF-268 in kinobead profiling experiments. d–f, Plots showing the correlation between pERK IC50 values of LXH-254 and LF-268 (d), AZ628 (e) and tovorafenib (f) in a panel of mutant KRAS-expressing cell lines. LXH-254 pERK IC50 values from Fig. 1e. LF-268, AZ628 and tovorafenib pERK IC50 values calculated from western blots in Extended Data Fig. 2b–d. Pearson's r values shown. pERK IC50 values and 95% CIs for d–f are listed in Supplementary Table 1. Source data We determined the potencies of AZ628, tovorafenib and LF-268 in blocking the activation of ERK in mutant KRAS-expressing cell lines that demonstrated a range of sensitivities to LXH-254. Consistent with their reported differential inhibitory potencies for RAF kinases18,19, these inhibitors exhibited a range of pERK IC50 values for the same mutant KRAS-expressing line (Extended Data Fig. 2a–d). Despite these differences, we found that the relative pERK IC50 values of AZ628, tovorafenib and LF-268 were highly correlated to those of LXH-254 (Fig. 2d–f). Thus, the cellular factors that modulate LXH-254's differential potency in different mutant KRAS-expressing lines similarly affect all DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors. We next investigated the mechanistic basis behind RAF's variable sensitivity to DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors across mutant KRAS-expressing lines. We found no correlation between KRAS mutant identity or zygosity and inhibitor sensitivity. For example, the most (HCT-116 and MDA-MB-231) and least (MDA-MB-453) sensitive cell lines to DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors are both heterozygous for KRAS-G13D (Figs. 1d,e and 2d–f). To determine whether basal signaling state influences RAF's sensitivity to inhibition, we measured the expression and phosphorylation levels of RAF–MEK–ERK pathway components across our comparative panel of six mutant KRAS-expressing lines to identify correlates of pERK IC50 values. We found limited correlation between pERK IC50 values and the relative A-RAF, B-RAF, C-RAF and MEK1/2 expression levels, the phosphorylation of RAF regulatory sites and RAF isoform DepMap scores (Extended Data Fig. 3a and Supplementary Fig. 3a–g). In contrast, basal pMEK and pERK levels were strongly inversely correlated with DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitor pERK IC50 values (Fig. 3a,b and Extended Data Fig. 3b,c). Thus, the more active RAF is in a mutant KRAS-expressing cell line, the more sensitive it is to inhibition by DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors (Fig. 3c). a,b, Correlation plots of pMEK (a) and pERK (b) levels versus LXH-254 (left) and tovorafenib (right) pERK IC50 values across a panel of mutant KRAS-expressing cell lines. Pearson's r values shown. Data points represent the mean of n = 3 biological replicates ± s.e.m. c, Illustration depicting the relationship between increased RAF activity within cells, as measured by increased pMEK, and enhanced sensitivity to inhibitors that stabilize the DFG-out conformation. d, Representative western blots (top) and associated pERK IC50 curves (bottom) from CIAR-293 cells treated with A115 or DMSO (1 h), followed by LXH-254 (3 h). Data points represent the mean of n = 3 biological replicates ± s.e.m. e, Representative western blot (top) and quantification (bottom) of normalized pMEK levels in CIAR-293 cells treated with A115 or DMSO (1 h). Data points represent the mean of n = 3 biological replicates ± s.e.m. f, Representative western blots (top) and B-RAF:C-RAF dimerization DC50 curves (bottom) from CIAR-293 cells treated with DMSO or A115 (1 h), followed by LXH-254 (3 h). Data points represent the mean of n = 3 biological replicates ± s.e.m. pERK IC50 curves from d are overlaid. g, Correlation plot of cellular LXH-254 B-RAF:C-RAF dimerization DC50s (from Extended Data Fig. 3f) and LXH-254 pERK IC50 values (from Fig. 1e). Error bars represent the s.e.m. pERK IC50 values, B-RAF:C-RAF dimerization DC50 values and 95% CIs for d,f are listed in Supplementary Table 1. Source data To determine whether elevated RAF activity correlates with DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitor sensitivity within the same cellular context, we measured LXH-254's potency in chemically inducible activator of RAS (CIAR)-293 cells. CIAR-293 cells facilitate the rapid formation of endogenous RAS–GTP, which promotes RAF dimerization and activation, in response to a small molecule, A115, that disrupts an autoinhibitory interaction within a membrane-localized CIAR protein switch (Extended Data Fig. 3d)33,34. We observed that LXH-254's pERK IC50 value was fourfold lower in A115-treated CIAR-293 cells compared to DMSO-treated CIAR-293 cells (Fig. 3d), directly corresponding to the fourfold higher pMEK levels upon A115 treatment (Fig. 3e). These results confirm that greater RAF activity enhances the potency of DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors. Inhibitor-promoted formation of RAF dimers is an important factor in assessing the potency of intracellular pathway inhibition. Like αC helix-out-stabilizing inhibitors, inhibitors that stabilize the DFG-out conformation promote single-inhibitor-occupied RAF dimers and downstream pathway signaling is only suppressed after occupation of the second protomer (Fig. 1a). Thus, residual pMEK and pERK at subsaturating DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitor concentrations could reflect uninhibited RAF dimers, single-inhibitor-occupied RAF dimers or a combination of both. We, therefore, assessed direct RAF engagement by measuring the inhibitor-promoted half-maximal dimerization constant (DC50) values of endogenous B-RAF and C-RAF through coimmunoprecipitations. We first asked whether LXH-254's lower pERK IC50 value in A115-treated CIAR-293 cells (Fig. 3d) corresponded to a similar decrease in heterodimerization DC50 value. Consistent with the notion that the ATP-binding site of more active RAF is easier to engage with DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors (Fig. 3c), we observed that lower concentrations of LXH-254 were required to promote 50% B-RAF:C-RAF heterodimerization in A115-treated versus DMSO-treated CIAR-293 cells (Fig. 3f and Supplementary Fig. 3g), although maximal heterodimerization levels were similar (Extended Data Fig. 3e). We also found that measured 50% pERK and 50% heterodimerization levels intersected at the same concentration of LXH-254 in both A115-treated and untreated CIAR-293 cells (Fig. 3f), suggesting that heterodimerization DC50 values represent engagement of the second protomer of B-RAF:C-RAF heterodimers. We next examined whether LXH-254's variable potency in inhibiting the RAF–MEK–ERK pathway across mutant KRAS-expressing cell lines corresponds with its ability to promote B-RAF:C-RAF heterodimerization. We treated five cell lines with variable sensitivities to DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors with a range of concentrations of LXH-254 and determined DC50 values in each by immunoprecipitating B-RAF and quantifying coenriched C-RAF. We observed that LXH-254-promoted B-RAF:C-RAF heterodimerization DC50 values (Extended Data Fig. 3f,g) spanned a wide range and correlated (r = 0.84) with pERK IC50 values (Fig. 3g). As in CIAR-293 cells, LXH-254 concentrations that provided 50% coimmunoprecipitation of C-RAF with B-RAF were similar to those yielding a 50% reduction in pERK levels in each line (Extended Data Fig. 3g). Size-exclusion chromatography (SEC) analysis of Mia PaCa-2 cell lysates showed that a saturating concentration of LXH-254 increased recruitment of B-RAF but not A-RAF into higher-molecular-weight fractions with C-RAF, consistent with dimer formation (Extended Data Fig. 4a). Accordingly, A-RAF was only recruited into heterodimers with C-RAF at LXH-254 concentrations (10–50 µM) where pERK levels decreased by >80% (Extended Data Fig. 4b). Thus, the variable ability of DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors to promote doubly occupied B-RAF:C-RAF heterodimers appears to be the primary determinant of RAF inhibitor sensitivity in mutant KRAS-expressing cell lines. When assessing the potency of inhibitors in suppressing signaling downstream of RAF dimers, two binding interactions—first and second protomer occupancy—must be considered. Inhibitors that stabilize the αC helix-out conformation, such as encorafenib and vemurafenib, exhibit a broad paradoxical activation concentration range because negative cooperativity between protomers necessitates much higher drug concentrations to achieve occupancy of the second protomer12,13. Consistent with a lack of negative cooperativity and potential positive cooperativity19 for DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors in occupying the second protomer of RAF dimers, we observed that cells treated with LXH-254 for 4 h demonstrated limited paradoxical activation compared to the αC helix-out-stabilizing encorafenib (Extended Data Fig. 4c,d). However, robust but transient paradoxical activation was observed at shorter LXH-254 treatment times (Extended Data Fig. 4e), consistent with LXH-254 initially promoting single-inhibitor-occupied, active RAF dimers from autoinhibited monomers before more potent second protomer engagement and inhibition ensue. To determine whether the potency of DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors for the second protomer of RAF dimers varies in different mutant KRAS-expressing cell lines, we generated single-inhibitor-occupied RAF dimers using encorafenib and measured LXH-254's pERK IC50 value as a readout of second protomer engagement (Fig. 4a). Encorafenib exhibits strong negative cooperativity between RAF protomers and slow dissociation kinetics (t½ > 24 h)35,36,37 enabling the quantitative formation of single-inhibitor-occupied RAF dimers by performing washout following cell treatment20. We first determined the optimal concentration of encorafenib for promoting maximal formation of single-inhibitor-occupied RAF dimers in HCT-116, Mia PaCa-2 and MDA-MB-453 cells (Extended Data Figs. 4c and 5a-c) and then quantified LXH-254's pERK IC50 values for single encorafenib-occupied RAF dimers in these same lines. We observed that encorafenib pretreatment and washout resulted in a modest 2–3-fold decrease in LXH-254's pERK IC50 value in HCT-116 cells but more dramatic ~30-fold and ~20-fold decreases in Mia PaCa-2 (Fig. 4b and Extended Data Fig. 5d) and MDA-MB-453 (Extended Data Fig. 5e) cells, respectively. Strikingly, despite the disparate sensitivities of these cell lines to DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors alone, LXH-254 potency in all three encorafenib-pretreated cell lines converged within a twofold range (pERK IC50 values = 28–50 nM). Collectively, this convergence suggests that the binding event that recruits monomeric RAF into dimers—first protomer occupation—is the primary determinant of cell line sensitivity to DFG-out-stabilizing RAF inhibitors. a, Method for measuring RAF dimer second protomer occupancy by DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors using encorafenib. b, pERK IC50 curves (quantified from western blots in Extended Data Fig. 5d) for HCT-116 and Mia PaCa-2 cells treated with encorafenib (1 h), followed by LXH-254 (3 h). Data points represent the mean of n = 3 biological replicates ± s.e.m. c, Representative western blots (bottom) and B-RAF:MEK CC50 curve (top) of MEK coimmunoprecipitated with B-RAF from Mia PaCa-2 cells treated with LXH-254 (3 h). Data points represent the mean of n = 3 biological replicates ± s.e.m. d, Representative western blots (top left) and quantification of B-RAF (top right), C-RAF (bottom left) and C-RAF pS259 (bottom right) coimmunoprecipitated with MEK from Mia PaCa-2 cells treated with 10 µM LXH-254 (4 h). Data points represent the mean of n = 3 biological replicates ± s.e.m. e, Structure of the antiparallel β-strand interaction between the C-terminal end of B-RAF's activation loop and MEK1's activation loop in the autoinhibited B-RAF:MEK1:14-3-3 complex (PDB 6NYB). The backbones of MEK1 (residues 223–226) and B-RAF (residues 613–616) are shown as gray and wheat sticks, respectively. Residues 613–616 from B-RAF in the LXH-254-bound complex (PDB 8F7P) are overlaid as light-blue sticks. f, Quantification of transiently expressed Myc–MEK1 coimmunoprecipitated with stably expressed WT or G615R mutant HA–B-RAF in CIAR-293 cells (representative western blots in Extended Data Fig. 6h). Data points represent the mean of n = 3 biological replicates ± s.e.m. g, Representative western blots (right) and pERK IC50 curves (left) from activated CIAR-293 cells stably expressing WT or G615R mutant HA–B-RAF treated with LXH-254 (4 h). Data points represent the mean of n = 3 biological replicates ± s.e.m. pERK IC50 values, RAF–MEK CC50 values and 95% CIs for b,c,g are listed in Supplementary Table 1. P values in d,f were determined using a two-sided Student's t-test. Source data Beyond recruiting B-RAF into high-molecular-weight fractions with C-RAF, our SEC analysis showed that LXH-254 treatment led to less MEK coeluting with B-RAF (Extended Data Fig. 6a) relative to untreated Mia PaCa-2 cells. Consistent with this observation, we found that LXH-254 dose-dependently decreased MEK coimmunoprecipitation with B-RAF (Fig. 4c and Supplementary Fig. 3g), with a 50% competition constant (CC50) value for MEK coenrichment similar to the B-RAF:C-RAF heterodimerization DC50 and pERK IC50 values in the same line (Extended Data Fig. 3g). LXH-254 also significantly decreased the amount of B-RAF and C-RAF that coenriched with immunoprecipitated MEK (Fig. 4d and Supplementary Fig. 4c), an effect shared by all four DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors (Extended Data Fig. 6b), suggesting a competitive relationship between DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitor binding and high-affinity B-RAF:MEK and C-RAF:MEK interactions. Notably, while we observed that LXH-254 dramatically decreased the total amount of C-RAF that coimmunoprecipitated with MEK, C-RAF phosphorylated at the S259 autoinhibitory site38 was less efficiently competed (Fig. 4d), consistent with LXH-254's limited engagement of the ATP-binding site of pS259 C-RAF (Supplementary Fig. 4a and Extended Data Fig. 6c,d) and lack of recruitment of pS259 C-RAF into heterodimers with B-RAF (Extended Data Fig. 6e). LXH-254 also did not modulate phosphorylation levels at the pS259 site (Extended Data Fig. 6f). Together, our data show that the binding of DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors to B-RAF and C-RAF antagonizes their high-affinity interactions with MEK, although the subpopulation of RAF phosphorylated at the autoinhibitory 14-3-3-binding site is likely a minor contributor to this competitive relationship. We compared the structure of the autoinhibited B-RAF:MEK:14-3-3 complex2 with structures of B-RAF bound to LXH-254, AZ628 or tovorafenib to identify a mechanistic rationale for how stabilizing the DFG-out conformation disrupts RAF's interactions with MEK. Previous structural studies revealed that autoinhibited B-RAF and C-RAF contain highly structured activation loops; residues following the DFG motif adopt an inhibitory turn stabilizing an outward αC-helix rotation, while the C-terminal end forms a short antiparallel β-strand interaction with MEK1's activation loop (Fig. 4e and Extended Data Fig. 6g)2,39. Rotation of the activation loop by stabilizing the DFG-out conformation disrupts the ordered inhibitory turn and likely destabilizes the antiparallel β-strand interaction between autoinhibited RAF and MEK (Fig. 4e). Therefore, we hypothesized that a mutation that destabilizes the antiparallel β-strand interaction between RAF and MEK would weaken high-affinity RAF–MEK interactions and enhance DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitor sensitivity by reducing the energetic cost of disrupting the autoinhibited complex. We focused on G615 in B-RAF (Fig. 4e), which is centered within the antiparallel β-strand interaction with MEK1 in the autoinhibited complex, adopts a backbone conformation only accessible to glycine and is the site of the clinically observed G615R substitution40. We generated a cell line stably expressing B-RAF-G615R and found that it promoted similar levels of ERK activation to WT B-RAF in activated CIAR-293 cells (Supplementary Fig. 4b) but coimmunoprecipitated MEK1 less efficiently (Fig. 4f and Extended Data Fig. 6h), consistent with disruption of B-RAF's antiparallel β-strand interaction with inactive MEK1. Supporting our hypothesis, we found that DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors more potently inhibited B-RAF-G615R-promoted pERK than WT B-RAF (Fig. 4g and Extended Data Fig. 7a). Our observation that B-RAF-G615H was comparably sensitized to LXH-254 demonstrates that this increase in potency is not because of a new affinity-enhancing inhibitor interaction with the introduced arginine side chain of G615R (Extended Data Fig. 7b). Together, these data support a model in which an RAF–MEK interaction that is specific to the autoinhibited complex antagonizes the formation of double-inhibitor-occupied RAF dimers. As MEK inhibitors have been shown to elicit varying effects on RAF–MEK interactions41,42,43, we investigated how pharmacological modulation of MEK affects RAF's sensitivity to DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors. We identified doses of five diverse MEK inhibitors (Fig. 5a) that achieved partial (~85%) MEK inhibition, measured as a decrease in pERK levels, in Mia PaCa-2 cells (Supplementary Fig. 5a) and measured LXH-254's potency following pretreatment with each. We found that four of the five MEK inhibitors tested either increased or had little effect on pERK IC50 values (Fig. 5b and Extended Data Fig. 7c–e). However, cobimetinib (10) pretreatment produced a ~4-fold increase in LXH-254's potency, suggesting that it uniquely promotes double-inhibitor-occupied RAF dimer formation, while other MEK inhibitors do not. To quantify the full extent of cobimetinib's sensitization of cells to RAF inhibition, we measured LXH-254's pERK IC50 in the least sensitive mutant KRAS-expressing line (MDA-MB-453) pretreated with a range of cobimetinib concentrations (Fig. 5c, Extended Data Fig. 7f and Supplementary Fig. 5b). Bliss analysis revealed significant synergy between LXH-254 and cobimetinib, with an average Bliss score of 8.72 (P < 0.05; Extended Data Fig. 7g). a, Structures of the clinical MEK inhibitors used in this study. b, pERK IC50 curves (quantified from western blots in Extended Data Fig. 7c,d) from Mia PaCa-2 cells treated with DMSO or partial inhibitory doses of cobimetinib or CH5126766 (1 h), then LXH-254 (3 h). Data points represent the mean of n = 3 biological replicates ± s.e.m. c, LXH-254 pERK IC50 curves (quantified from western blots in Extended Data Fig. 7f) from MDA-MB-453 cells treated with a range of cobimetinib doses (1 h) and then LXH-254 (4 h). Data points represent the mean of n = 3 biological replicates ± s.e.m. d, Correlation plot of LXH-254 pERK IC50 values from c and pMEK levels (quantified from western blots in Extended Data Fig. 7f) from cobimetinib-pretreated MDA-MB-453 cells. Pearson's r value is shown. e, Representative western blots (top) and quantification (bottom) of A-RAF and B-RAF coimmunoprecipitated with MEK from Mia PaCa-2 cells treated with partial inhibitory doses of cobimetinib, GDC-0623 or CH5126766 (4 h). Data points represent the mean of n = 3 biological replicates ± s.e.m. f, Representative western blots (left) and LXH-254 B-RAF:C-RAF DC50 curves (right) from Mia PaCa-2 cells pretreated with DMSO or partial inhibitory doses of cobimetinib or CH5126766 (1 h), followed by LXH-254 (3 h). Data points represent the mean of n = 3 biological replicates ± s.e.m. B-RAF:C-RAF DC50 curve for DMSO pretreatment from Extended Data Fig. 3f,g. g, Structure of the activation loops of B-RAF (cyan) and MEK1 (wheat) in the GDC-0623-bound B-RAF:MEK1 complex (PDB 6PP9). The activation loop of cobimetinib-bound MEK1 (dark gray; PDB 7MOV) is overlaid, demonstrating how movement of the α-helical region of MEK1's activation loop would alter autoinhibitory interactions with B-RAF. pERK IC50 values, B-RAF:C-RAF DC50 values and 95% CIs for b,c,f are listed in Supplementary Table 1. Source data To investigate the mechanistic basis of cobimetinib's unique sensitizing behavior, we compared its effects on RAF interactions to those of CH5126766 (7), an MEK inhibitor that potently antagonizes LXH-254's inhibitory activity (Fig. 5b) and previous studies have shown to promote stable RAF–MEK complexes44. We found that cobimetinib pretreatment led to >10-fold lower pERK IC50 values relative to CH5126766 for AZ628, tovorafenib and LF-268 (Extended Data Fig. 7h–j), indicating that the potency-modulating effects of these MEK inhibitors extend to all DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors. Consistent with divergent modulation of autoinhibited RAF complexes and previous results42,43, cobimetinib greatly enhanced MEK activation, whereas CH5126766 either reduced or minimally affected pMEK relative to DMSO across multiple mutant KRAS-expressing lines (Extended Data Fig. 8a). No consistent trends were observed for B-RAF and C-RAF regulatory phosphorylation sites (Extended Data Fig. 8b). Notably, cobimetinib's promotion of elevated pMEK levels (Extended Data Fig. 7f) strongly correlated with its capacity to lower LXH-254 pERK IC50 values (Fig. 5d), suggesting that cobimetinib-promoted pMEK elevations reflect increases in DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitor-sensitive RAF dimers. Consistent with this, cobimetinib, like LXH-254, increased TurboID–KRAS proximity labeling of all RAF isoforms in activated CIAR-293 cells compared to DMSO or a nonsensitizing MEK inhibitor (Extended Data Fig. 8c–e). Consistent with their opposing effects on the formation of double-inhibitor-occupied RAF dimers, cobimetinib disrupted while CH5126766 promoted high-affinity RAF–MEK interactions (Fig. 5e and Supplementary Fig. 4c), with interaction levels correlating with LXH-254 pERK IC50 values (Extended Data Fig. 9a). Further confirming cobimetinib's disruption of high-affinity RAF–MEK interactions, SEC analysis of cobimetinib-treated Mia PaCa-2 cells showed less MEK coelution with B-RAF and C-RAF in high-molecular-weight fractions (Extended Data Fig. 9b,c). We found that these divergent effects on high-affinity RAF–MEK interactions were inversely related to each MEK inhibitor's capacity to modulate LXH-254-promoted B-RAF:C-RAF heterodimerization (Fig. 5f and Extended Data Fig. 9d,e). Specifically, cobimetinib-pretreated cells required lower concentrations of LXH-254 to promote heterodimerization, whereas CH5126766-pretreated cells required higher concentrations. Together, these data support a model in which MEK inhibitors modulate RAF's recruitment into DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitor-promoted dimers by tuning autoinhibited complex interaction strength. What underlies cobimetinib's unique ability to reduce MEK's interaction with autoinhibited RAF kinases? Structural comparison of MEK inhibitor-bound B-RAF:MEK1 complexes revealed high overall structural similarity (Extended Data Fig. 9f). However, the conformation of an α-helix in the N-terminal region of inactive MEK1's activation loop in the cobimetinib-bound complex was unique among all MEK inhibitor-bound structures (Extended Data Fig. 9g). This α-helix precedes the portion of MEK1's activation loop that forms an antiparallel β-strand interaction with autoinhibited B-RAF's activation loop and its inward rotation influences interactions between inactive B-RAF and MEK1 (Fig. 5g). We speculate that cobimetinib promotes a conformation of the α-helical region of inactive MEK's activation loop that reduces its interaction strength with autoinhibited RAF kinase activation loops, thereby facilitating DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitor-promoted RAF dimer formation. We predicted that cobimetinib derivatives with enhanced disruption of high-affinity RAF–MEK interactions could be generated to further increase RAF's sensitivity to DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors. To guide analog generation, we compared cocrystal structures of the autoinhibited B-RAF:MEK1 complex bound to cobimetinib and the structurally similar, nonsensitizing MEK inhibitor GDC-0623 (6) (Fig. 5a and Extended Data Fig. 9g). While both inhibitors form comparable contacts with a hydrophobic pocket in MEK1 through their biaryl anilines, their alcohol-containing substituents interact divergently with the α-helical region of inactive MEK1's activation loop (Fig. 6a)41. We hypothesized that cobimetinib's piperidinyl-4-azetidinol substituent promotes an inward rotation of the α-helix in MEK1's activation loop that weakens its interaction with autoinhibited RAF (Fig. 5g) and that this region of the inhibitor could be further optimized. Therefore, we generated a panel of cobimetinib analogs (11–14) and purchased a MEK inhibitor (15) with varied substituents replacing the piperidinyl-4-azetidinol moiety (Fig. 6b), determined partial (~85%) inhibitory doses for each and tested their ability to sensitize Mia PaCa-2 cells to LXH-254 (Extended Data Fig. 10a,b). We found that partial inhibitory doses of these analogs yielded a range of LXH-254 pERK IC50 values, with compound 13 providing the greatest sensitization and compound 15 providing the least (Fig. 6c,d). Thus, the identity of the substituent that extends toward the α-helical region of MEK's activation loop substantially influences sensitization. a, Superimposed cocrystal structures of cobimetinib-bound (gray; PDB 7MOV) and GDC-0623-bound (wheat; PDB 6NYB) MEK1 showing the α-helical region of MEK1's activation loop. b, Structures of MEK inhibitors 11–15 and cobimetinib. c,d, Representative western blots (c) and pERK IC50 curves (d) from Mia PaCa-2 cells pretreated with partial inhibitory doses of 11–15 or cobimetinib (1 h), followed by LXH-254 (3 h) Cobimetinib and DMSO pretreatment curves are from Fig. 5b. Data points represent the mean of n = 3 biological replicates. pERK IC50 values = 420 nM (11), 300 nM (12), 68 nM (13), 170 nM (14), 1,300 nM (15) and 590 nM (DMSO). e, Correlation plot of LXH-254 pERK IC50 values from d and pMEK levels (quantified from western blots in Extended Data Fig. 10c) in Mia PaCa-2 cells treated with partial inhibitory doses of 11–15 (4 h). Pearson's r value is shown. pMEK levels represent the mean of n = 3 biological replicates ± s.e.m. pERK IC50 error bars represent 95% CIs. f,g, Representative western blots (f) and quantification (g) of B-RAF and C-RAF coimmunoprecipitated with MEK1 from Mia PaCa-2 cells treated with partial inhibitory doses of inhibitors 11–15 or cobimetinib (4 h). Data points represent the mean of n = 3 biological replicates ± s.e.m. h, Correlation plot of pERK IC50 values from d and mean B-RAF and C-RAF levels coimmunoprecipitated with MEK1 from f,g. Pearson's r values are shown. Error bars for coimmunoprecipitated B-RAF and C-RAF levels represent the s.e.m. pERK IC50 error bars represent 95% CIs. i, Model showing how MEK inhibitors that destabilize high-affinity RAF–MEK interactions sensitize RAF to DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors. pERK IC50 values and 95% CIs for d are listed in Supplementary Table 1. Source data We next determined whether the varying levels of sensitization provided by compounds 11–15 relate to their ability to promote RAF dimerization through disruption of high-affinity RAF–MEK interactions. Partial inhibitory doses of compounds 11–15 produced varying degrees of RAF dimerization, as indicated by pMEK levels (Extended Data Fig. 10c), with an MEK inhibitor's ability to elevate pMEK correlating with its capacity to lower LXH-254 pERK IC50 values (Fig. 6e). We next assessed the ability of compounds 11–15 to directly disrupt high-affinity RAF–MEK interactions by immunoprecipitating MEK from Mia PaCa-2 cells treated with partial inhibitory doses of each and probing for coenriched B-RAF and C-RAF. We observed that compounds 11–15 varied in their capacity to disrupt MEK's interaction with B-RAF and C-RAF (Fig. 6f,g). Importantly, RAF–MEK coimmunoprecipitation levels strongly correlated with LXH-254 pERK IC50 values across cells cotreated with compounds 11–15 (Fig. 6h), consistent with RAF–MEK disruption as a key mechanism of sensitization. Thus, MEK inhibitors tuned to disrupt autoinhibitory RAF–MEK complexes both increase RAF activity and sensitize it to DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors, consistent with a model in which high-affinity RAF–MEK interactions antagonize DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitor occupancy (Fig. 6i). Several RAF inhibitors stabilizing the αC helix-out conformation have been approved for Erdheim–Chester disease and B-RAF V600-driven melanomas45,46. However, these inhibitors poorly suppress MEK/ERK activation in cells expressing mutant RAS or non-V600 B-RAF mutants because of paradoxical activation from single-inhibitor-occupied RAF dimers11,12,13. Inhibitors stabilizing the DFG-out conformation overcome this limitation and suppress MEK/ERK activation more broadly because they lack negative cooperativity between inhibitor-bound protomers18,19,20, although their efficacy in suppressing downstream pathway activation has been observed to vary in different cellular contexts12,21,22,23,24,25,26,27. We found a strong correlation between cellular RAF kinase activity and RAF's sensitivity to DFG-out inhibition across mutant KRAS-expressing cell lines, using a panel of four structurally diverse inhibitors that stabilize this conformation—three of which have been structurally validated and one (LF-268) that has not. Our data showing that DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors recruit B-RAF and C-RAF into heterodimers by disrupting their autoinhibited complexes with inactive MEK (Figs. 3 and 4) indicate that cellular sensitivity depends on how readily these high-affinity autoinhibited complexes are disrupted. We also found that MEK inhibitors differentially modulate RAF inhibitor sensitivity on the basis of their effects on RAF–MEK interactions, enabling us to design MEK inhibitors that enhance sensitization to DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors. The signaling basis for differential RAF activity across mutant KRAS-expressing lines remains unclear. RAS–GTP levels modulate RAF kinase activity and we and others42,47 have shown that increased RAS activation sensitizes cells to DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors in the same cellular context, yet neither KRAS mutant identity nor zygosity correlated with basal pMEK/pERK levels (Figs. 1d,e, 2d and 3a). Differential A-RAF, B-RAF and C-RAF proficiencies in phosphorylating MEK48 mean that dimer composition also impacts overall RAF kinase activity, yet relative RAF isoform expression levels and regulatory phosphorylation status were not predictive of pMEK levels. We observed that C-RAF phosphorylated at its major autoinhibitory site (S259) is minimally recruited into inhibitor-promoted heterodimers (Extended Data Fig. 6e), which is also likely true for A-RAF and B-RAF. Thus, these subpopulations of RAF, which are stably associated with inactive MEK and two 14-3-3 proteins and can only be recruited into active dimers after dephosphorylation by the SHOC2:PP1C phosphatase complex49, likely do not contribute to DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitor sensitivity. Therefore, we speculate that RAF activity in mutant KRAS-expressing cells is governed by a combination of RAS–GTP levels and the balance of RAF homodimers and heterodimers recruited by active RAS. We performed a systematic analysis of DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitor sensitivity in mutant KRAS-expressing cell lines but it is unknown whether the trends we observed generalize to cancer cells that express other mutant RAS isoforms and/or B-RAF mutants. AZ628 has been shown to have a stronger effect on cell viability as a single agent in mutant NRAS-expressing cells than cells that express mutant KRAS42 and we speculate that this is because of comparably higher basal RAF kinase activity and an increased ability of DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors to block MEK and ERK activation. However, this conjecture needs to be validated. While αC helix-out-stabilizing inhibitors effectively suppress B-RAF-V600E-driven signaling, they are less effective against non-V600 mutants. Our finding that B-RAF-G615R is sensitized to DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors suggests that other B-RAF mutations disrupting autoinhibitory RAF–MEK interactions may share this property. The low pERK IC50 values across all four DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors in MDA-MB-231 cells (Fig. 2d–f), corresponding to the only mutant KRAS-expressing line we profiled that coexpresses an RAF mutant (B-RAF-G464V), suggest that mutations not directly disrupting RAF–MEK interactions may also confer sensitivity, likely by promoting higher basal RAF dimerization50 and bypassing the potency-limiting step of recruiting monomeric RAF into dimers. Whether DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors will act as potent single agents or require synergistic MEK inhibitor cotreatment will depend on the specifics of pathway dysregulation. We profiled how diverse MEK inhibitors affect RAF's intracellular interactions and sensitivity to DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors using short cotreatment regimens and concentrations of each that provided a similar level of partial (85%) downstream inhibition. This level of downstream inhibition was selected because it facilitated the engagement of a significant population of MEK while maintaining a sufficient amount of pathway activity where synergy could be observed. We found that cobimetinib was unique in providing sensitization to DFG-out-stabilizing RAF inhibitors, which is consistent with its promotion of B-RAF:C-RAF heterodimers and disruption of high-affinity RAF–MEK interactions2. Our results are concordant with a study showing that cobimetinib was the most synergistic MEK inhibitor when combined with AZ628 in cell viability assays across multiple mutant KRAS-expressing cell lines; the study also showed that, under longer cotreatment regimens, cobimetinib allowed AZ628 to more potently block ERK activation in A549 cells42. Several MEK inhibitors that were not in our panel have also demonstrated synergy, albeit less than cobimetinib, with AZ628 in cell viability assays under prolonged cotreatment regimens42,51 and further studies are warranted to determine whether they provide sensitization by a similar mechanism as cobimetinib. Cobimetinib's superior synergistic effects with AZ628 in cell viability assays suggest that our analogs with increased sensitization properties may also provide greater synergy when combined with DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors in blocking the proliferation of mutant KRAS-expressing cells. We found that occupation of B-RAF's and C-RAF's ATP-binding sites with DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors recruits them into dimers from autoinhibited, monomeric complexes. Our results suggest that MEK forms higher-affinity interactions with inactive, monomeric B-RAF and C-RAF, which are likely mediated by autoinhibitory activation loop interactions, than within dimeric RAF complexes. On the basis of this mechanistic observation, we generated cobimetinib analogs that we predicted would differentially modulate the conformation of an α-helix in the N-terminal region of inactive MEK's activation loop. We observed that the degree to which these structurally similar inhibitors disrupted autoinhibitory RAF–MEK interactions directly correlated with their sensitization of RAF to DFG-out-stabilizing inhibitors. Although we identified an MEK inhibitor that more effectively disrupts high-affinity RAF–MEK interactions and provides greater sensitization than cobimetinib, the molecular details for how it achieves these properties are not known. Future structural studies of MEK inhibitor-bound RAF–MEK complexes will provide insight into how these analogs modulate MEK's activation loop and guide further optimization. Together, our findings provide mechanistic insights into the sensitivity of RAF kinases to DFG-out inhibition and demonstrate how cellular signaling state impacts inhibitor potency. How these effects on direct pathway inhibition translate to antiproliferative activity under prolonged treatment remains to be determined; however, our work supports further development of MEK inhibitors optimally tuned to synergize with DFG-out-stabilizing RAF inhibitors. AZ628 (HY-11004), tovorafenib (HY-15246), cobimetinib (HY-13064), binimetinib (HY-15202), CH5126766 (HY-18652), trametinib (HY-10999), encorafenib (HY-15605) and GDC-0623 (HY-15610) were obtained from MedChemExpress. Danoprevir was obtained from ApexBio (A4024). A-1155463 was obtained from ChemieTek (CT-A115). LXH-254 (S8745) and vemurafenib (S1267) were obtained from Selleck Chemicals. Compound 15 (3,4-difluoro-2-[(2-fluoro-4-iodophenyl)amino]-N-(2-hydroxyethoxy)benzamide) was obtained from AABlocks (AA01EMTY). We verified that commercial inhibitors were >98% pure by liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC–MS) analysis (200–352 nm). The following antibodies were used: MEK1 61B12 mouse monoclonal antibody (mAb; Cell Signaling Technology, 2352; 1:500 dilution), phospho-p44/42 MAPK (ERK1/2) D13.14.4E XP rabbit mAb (Cell Signaling Technology, 4370; 1:1,000 dilution), p44/42 MAPK (ERK1/2) (3A7) mouse mAb (Cell Signaling Technology, 9107; 1:1,000 dilution), phospho-MEK1/2 (S217/S221) (41G9) rabbit mAb (Cell Signaling Technology, 9154), C-RAF (D4B3J) rabbit mAb (Cell Signaling Technology, 53745; 1:1,000 dilution), phospho-C-RAF (S338) (56A6) rabbit mAb (Cell Signaling Technology, 9427; 1:1,000 dilution), phospho-C-RAF (S259) antibody (Cell Signaling Technology, 9421; 1:1,000 dilution), phospho-C-RAF (S289/S296/S301) antibody (Cell Signaling Technology, 9431; 1:1,000 dilution), phospho-B-RAF (S445) antibody (Cell Signaling Technology, 2696; 1:1,000 dilution), phospho-C-RAF (S621) polyclonal antibody (Invitrogen, 44-504G; 1:500 dilution), protein A agarose beads (Cell Signaling Technology, 9863), protein G agarose beads (Cell Signaling Technology, 37478), 14-3-3 (pan) E9S9M rabbit mAb (Cell Signaling Technology, 95422; 1:1,000 dilution), B-RAF antibody (F-7) (Santa Cruz Biotechnology, 5284; 1:200 dilution), anti-phospho-B-RAF (T401) antibody produced in rabbit (Sigma-Aldrich, SAB5701867; 1:1,000 dilution), A-RAF antibody (A-5) (Santa Cruz Biotechnology, 166771; 1:200 dilution), anti-MEK1 antibody (Sigma-Aldrich, 07-641; 1:500 dilution), purified mouse anti-MEK1 clone 25/MEK1 (BD Biosciences, 610122; 1:500 dilution), GAPDH antibody (A-3) (Santa Cruz Biotechnology, 137179; 1:2,000 dilution), mouse IgG2a isotype control (PPV-04) (Thermo Scientific, Invitrogen, MA1-10418), normal rabbit IgG (Sigma-Aldrich, 12-370), IRDye 680RD goat anti-mouse IgG secondary antibody (LI-COR Biosciences, 926-68070; 1:10,000 dilution), IRDye 800CW goat anti-rabbit IgG secondary antibody (LI-COR Biosciences, 926-32211; 1:10,000 dilution) and Pierce anti-HA magnetic beads (Thermo Fisher Scientific, 88836). All plasmids were constructed using Gibson assembly and standard protocols and then validated through Sanger or whole-plasmid sequencing. The following primers were used: B-RAF WT: CGTCGTCAGGATCATCGCCCTTAAGCTTGCCGCCACCatgggaTAT, AACGGGCCCTCTAGATGCATGCTCGAGtcagtggacaggaaacgcaccat; B-RAF-G615R: CGTCGTCAGGATCATCGCCCTTAAGCTTGCCGCCACCatgggaTAT, AACGGGCCCTCTAGATGCATGCTCGAGtcagtggacaggaaacgcaccat, ccatcagtttgaacagttgtctCGAtccattttgtggatggcac agacaactgttcaaactgatgg; TurboID–KRAS TurboID fragment: atggaacaaaaactcatctcagaagaggatctcggtggaaaagacaatactgtgcctct, AGCCAGATCCactaccgctgccaccctgcagcttttcggcag; TurboID–KRAS KRAS fragment: agcggtagtGGATCTGGCTCAGGAatgactgaatataaacttgtggtagttg, GTTTAAACGGGCCCTCTAGACTCGAGttacataattacacactttgtctttgact. All cancer cell lines used in this study, namely Mia PaCa-2 (CRL-1420), MDA-MB-453 (HTB-131), SW-480 (CCL-228), DLD-1 (CCL-221), MDA-MB-231 (HTB-26), HCT-116 (CCL-247), LoVo (CCL-229), HPAF-II (CRL-1997), AsPc-1 (CRL-1682), A549 (CCL-185) and Calu-6 (HTB-56) were purchased from the American Type Culture collection (ATCC) and cultured in DMEM (Thermo Fisher) + 10% FBS (Millipore Sigma), with the exception of HCT-116 cells, which were cultured in McCoy's medium (Thermo Fisher) supplemented with 10% FBS. All cell lines were authenticated by ATCC through short tandem repeat profiling. All cell lines were maintained at 37 °C with 5% CO2. CIAR-293 expressing cell lines were generated as previously described33,52. Gel-ready samples were quickly centrifuged and loaded onto any-kDa SDS–PAGE gels (Bio-Rad). Then, 2 µl of PAGEruler Plus prestained protein ladder (Thermo Scientific) was loaded onto each gel. SDS–PAGE was performed at 200 V for 40 min. Gels were then placed into Trans-Blot Turbo nitrocellulose midi transfer packs (Bio-Rad) and loaded into a Trans-Blot Turbo (Bio-Rad) before performing protein transfer (16 V, 14 min). Membranes were then incubated in blocking buffer (5% dry milk in 1× Tris-buffered saline (TBS) and 0.1% Tween-20 (TBST)) for 1 h at room temperature, followed by an overnight incubation at 4 °C with primary antibodies. Blots were then washed three times with TBST and incubated with IRDye 680RD goat anti-mouse IgG secondary antibody (LI-COR Biosciences) and IRDye 800CW goat anti-rabbit IgG secondary antibody (LI-COR Biosciences) for 1 h in blocking buffer at room temperature. Blots were washed three times with TBST and imaged on a LI-COR Odyssey Dlx. Western blot analysis and quantification were performed using ImageStudio software. Cells were plated on 12-well plates and allowed to adhere and grow overnight. Inhibitor incubations were started when cells were 80–90% confluent for 4-h incubations or at 50% confluence for 24-h incubations. All inhibitor titrations were performed in FBS-containing medium. Cells were treated with LXH-254, LF-268, AZ628 (initial concentration: 10 µM, threefold serial dilutions, final [DMSO] = 0.05%) or tovorafenib (initial concentration: 10 µM (HCT-116 and SW-480) or 50 µM (Mia PaCa-2, MDA-MB-453, MDA-MB-231 and DLD-1), threefold serial dilutions, final [DMSO] = 0.05%) for 4 or 24 h at 37 °C. Cells were lysed using a modified RIPA buffer containing 50 mM Tris-HCl pH 7.5, 150 mM NaCl, 10% glycerol, 1% IGEPAL, phosphatase inhibitor cocktails 2 + 3 (Sigma-Aldrich) and protease inhibitor tablet (Thermo Scientific). Cells were incubated in the buffer for 30 min at 4 °C with agitation. The lysate was cleared by centrifugation at 17,000g for 10 min at 4 °C and the supernatant was collected. Next, 4× SDS loading dye containing 200 mM Tris pH 6.8, 8% SDS, 40% glycerol, 4% β-mercaptoethanol and 0.08% bromophenol blue were added to each sample, followed by vortexing and heating at 95 °C for 5 min. Eluted proteins were subjected to SDS–PAGE and western blot analysis using p44/42 MAPK (ERK1/2) (3A7) mouse mAb (Cell Signaling Technology), phospho-p44/42 MAPK (ERK1/2) D13.14.4E XP rabbit mAb (Cell Signaling Technology) or phospho-MEK1/2 (S217/S221) (41G9) rabbit mAb (Cell Signaling Technology) and purified mouse anti-MEK1 clone 25/MEK1 (BD Biosciences) antibodies at 4 °C. Lysis buffer and washing buffer 1 consisted of 50 mM Tris pH 7.8, 120 mM NaCl, 10 mM NaF, 1 mM Na3VO4, 1 mM EDTA and 1% (v/v) IGEPAL CA-630. Washing buffer 2 consisted of 50 mM Tris pH 7.8, 120 mM NaCl and 1 mM EDTA. Denaturing buffer consisted of 50 mM Tris pH 8.5, 6 M guanidinium chloride, 5 mM TCEP (added fresh) and 10 mM CAM (added fresh). Pierce protease inhibitor tablets (A32963) and the Pierce 660-nm protein assay were obtained from Thermo Scientific. NHS-activated Sepharose 4 fast flow resin was obtained from GE Healthcare (17090601). LysC lysyl endopeptidase (MS-grade) was obtained from Wako. MS-grade trypsin was obtained from Pierce. Cells were grown on 10-cm plates in DMEM supplemented with 10% (by volume) FBS at 37 °C in a humidified incubator containing 5% CO2 until 90% confluence. The 268-TCO probe was added to the cells by replacing medium with fresh DMEM (5 ml) containing 10% FBS and 268-TCO (5 µM) or DMSO (0.1% in the final medium). Cells were incubated with 268-TCO for 30 min at 37 °C in the incubator and the medium was removed immediately after incubation. The cells were quickly washed with ice-cold Dulbecco's PBS (DPBS) (5 ml, one time) and immediately lysed with ice-cold lysis buffer (500 µl). Cells were scraped into prechilled 1.5-ml Eppendorf tubes and were centrifuged at 17,000g for 20 min at 4 °C. The supernatant of lysates was collected and protein concentration was measured using the Pierce 660-nm protein assay. The protein concentration of lysates was diluted to 1.5 mg ml−1 with ice-cold lysis buffer. Tz-Beads were generated according to previously described protocol32. To reduce background contaminants, lysates were precleared by incubating with 15 µl (volume of drained beads) of Sepharose beads coupled to ethanolamine for 1 h at 4 °C before incubation with Tz-beads. For each pulldown experiment, 15 µl (volume of drained beads) of Tz-Beads were added to a 1.5-ml Eppendorf tube and were washed with ice-cold lysis buffer (three times; 400 µl for each wash). Next, 750 µg of protein lysates (1.5 mg ml−1) were added to Tz-Beads (15 µl) and was gently incubated on an end-to-end rotator for 30 mins at 4 °C. After incubation with Tz-Beads, the flowthrough was quickly removed by aspiration. The beads were then washed with ice-cold washing buffer 1 by gently inverting the tube four times (three times; 400 µl for each wash), followed by washing with washing buffer 2 by gently inverting the tube for four times (three times; 400 µl for each wash). The target protein should be enriched and bound to the Tz-Beads by the end of this step. To detect enriched targets using LC–MS, the washed beads were resuspended in 50 µl of denaturing buffer and were boiled at 95 °C for 5 min. After cooling down, 100 mM TEAB (50 µl) was added. The pH value was adjusted to 8–9 by adding NaOH (1 M). LysC lysyl endopeptidase (1 µg) was then added and incubated with the beads at 37 °C for 2 h in a thermal mixer agitating at 1,400 rpm. After incubation, the suspension was diluted with 100 mM TEAB (100 µl) and MS-grade trypsin (1 µg) was added. The pH was adjusted to 8–9. The beads were incubated with trypsin overnight at 37 °C in a thermal mixer agitating at 1,400 rpm. After incubation, buffer A (200 µl) was added to the beads and trypsin was quenched by adjusting the pH to 2–3 using formic acid. The resulting supernatant was desalted on C18 StageTip and washed once with buffer A (50 µl). The tryptic peptides were eluted off the C18 StageTip using buffer B (50 µl). The eluted solution was dried in a SpeedVac and then reconstituted with buffer A (20 µl) for LC–MS analysis. Peptides were separated on a nanoAcquity ultrahigh-performance LC (UPLC) instrument with a 12-cm long fused silica capillary column (Polymicro Technologies, 1068150019; flexible fused silica capillary tubing, inner diameter: 75 µm, outer diameter: 375 µm) made in-house with a laser puller and packed with 5 µm of 120-Å reverse-phase C18 beads (ReproSil-Pur 120 C18-AQ, Dr. Maisch HPLC, r15.aq). The LC gradient was 90 min long with 10–35% B at 200 nl min−1. LC solvent A was 0.1% acetic acid and LC solvent B was 0.1% acetic acid and 99.9% acetonitrile.
