‘American Idol' Season 24 Finale Spoilers: Everything We Know So Far
Source: EntertainmentNow    Published: 2026-05-12 03:25:31

Tonight is the season 24 finale of “American Idol,” and we already have some “American Idol” spoilers on what to expect. Read below for a rundown on what we know so far for the “American Idol” 2026 finale. WARNING: If you do NOT want to know anything about the “American Idol” season 24 finale, STOP READING NOW. Since this is the Grand Finale, we have a supersized episode of “American Idol” tonight. The night kicks off at 8/7c on ABC, like normal. It's going to be a jam-packed night of performances. In addition to the Top 3 performing live for your votes, we will see a lot of musical guests and familiar faces from Season 24 back on that stage. To make up for this jam-packed night, ABC is giving us three full hours of Idol tonight. The winner of “American Idol” season 24 will be announced by Ryan Seacrest around 11 p.m. ET tonight, as the night and season come to an end. As stated above, we are going to have a big night of performances. The Top 3 — Hannah Harper, Jordan McCullough, and Keyla Richardson — will take the stage and perform for your votes one last night. For the Grand Finale night, we are also going to have Alicia Keys serving as a guest mentor and judge for the Top 3. In addition, Keys will take to the stage for a special performance during the finale night. ABC has also announced the following musical guests for the Idol Finale: We always know that eliminated contestants from the current season will be coming back for the finale. The show has shared on their Instagram pages some of these performers. We will see Jesse Findling and Jake Thistle perform with Blues Traveler and Gin Blossoms. We will also see Braden Rumfelt perform a duet with Clay Aiken. Lucas Leon will be performing with Brad Paisley. Lee Ann Womack will be performing with Hannah Harper. Jason Mraz performing with Keyla Richardson. Tori Kelly performing with Jordan McCullough and Daniel Stallworth. The “American Idol” judges will all take the stage and perform together. In addition, Carrie Underwood will be taking the stage to perform with Mötley Crüe. Are you ready to vote for your favorite singer? America gets the final say in who wins “American Idol” season 24 tonight. To vote, there are three ways to do it: Live voting will end during the final commercial break, and the winner will be announced during the final moments of the live finale. The “American Idol” season 29 finale airs tonight at 8/7c on ABC with streaming available on Hulu the next day. Like EntertainmentNow? Meghan Markle shares sweet snaps from a Disneyland family celebration, including kids and mom Doria
Source: Page Six    Published: 2026-05-12 02:41:00

Just a day after Mother's Day, Meghan Markle shared an Instagram carousel of photos showing off a fun family day at Disneyland with her kids Archie and Lilibet, husband, Prince Harry, and her mom, Doria Ragland. It's not clear if the clan actually visited the famed California theme park on Sunday — Mother's Day — or a different date, but the sunny pics show off a happy time. The selection of pics shared on Monday begins with Markle giving her mom a kiss on the cheek — both sporting Mickey ears — while the kids play behind them with a cluster of mouse-ear balloons. Advertisement Markle is then seen from behind with Harry and the children, walking through the California Adventure park. She then shares a picture of Lilibet visiting with costumed Disney princesses, while Archie sneakily hides behind a post. Advertisement Lilibet accepts a hug from Cinderella in another shot, and then she and her brother are shown on the Storybook Land Canal Boats enjoying the scenery. The collection of pictures ends with the family wandering through Star Wars: Galaxy's Edge as a Stormtrooper looks on. Advertisement Start your day with Page Six Daily. Please provide a valid email. By clicking above you agree to the Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. Want celebrity news as it breaks? The Disneyland outing may possibly have been for Archie's birthday, which fell on May 6, just several days before Mother's Day. Markle marked her and Prince Harry's eldest child's 7th year with a touching Instagram tribute on that day last week. “7 years later…happy birthday to our sweet boy 🤍” the “Suits” alum, 44, captioned a photo of Harry cradling Archie as a newborn. The Duke and Duchess of Sussex welcomed Archie in May 2019. Advertisement During the infant's debut, the new mom dubbed motherhood “magic” and “pretty amazing.” Princess Lilibet, now 4, joined the family in June 2021. By that time, Harry, 41, and Markle had resigned from their royal roles and moved to a Montecito, California, mansion — only a couple hours' drive from Anaheim, California, where Disneyland is located.

Q1 2026 Market Share: REPUBLIC Leads, Atlantic Jumps to Second, Sony Narrows Gap On UMG
Source: Billboard    Published: 2026-05-12 01:17:53

By label ownership, the independent sector commanded more than 44% of the U.S. market in the first quarter of 2026, according to Billboard estimates based on Luminate data. By Dan Rys A combination of factors — including a surging independent sector, a varied release schedule and some big albums shifting from current status to catalog — has made the first quarter of 2026 an aberration in comparison to the recent history of U.S. recorded-music market share. Still, some things don't change: As has been the case for much of the past half-decade, big albums from Taylor Swift (The Life of a Showgirl) and Morgan Wallen (I'm the Problem) helped drive REPUBLIC to an industry-leading market share, ending the first quarter with a 9.98% current share, defined as albums released in the past 18 months, according to Luminate. (REPUBLIC's market share includes Island, Big Loud, Mercury and Universal Records.) Yet those fluctuations in the quarter — as well as Swift's 1989 (Taylor's Version) and The Tortured Poets Department; Wallen's One Thing at a Time; and Drake's For All the Dogs all moving to catalog status — meant that REPUBLIC's current share slipped below 10% in a quarter for the first time since 2022. Also shaking things up is Atlantic Music Group, which has capitalized on growing momentum over the past year to surge into second place, with an 8.76% share — its highest current market share since 2022, when it regularly posted figures north of 9%. (Atlantic's market share also includes 300 Entertainment and 10K Projects.) Big projects from Bruno Mars and Don Toliver and continued success from singles by Kehlani and Alex Warren helped boost Atlantic to the No. 2 slot. Year-over-year comparisons for Atlantic are tough, as 10K, which on its own has a 1.69% current share this year, was not incorporated into its market share until the third quarter of 2025. Dropping to third is Interscope Geffen A&M, at a 7.95% share, a reflection of both its release schedule and two major releases of its own shifting to catalog: Billie Eilish's Hit Me Hard and Soft and Gracie Abrams' The Secret of Us. (IGA's market share also includes Verve.) That's down from a current score of 12.67% in last year's first quarter, when it was the top label in the U.S. industry. All that upheaval among current titles at the top means that Universal Music Group's current market share by distribution ownership dipped to 30.76% for the first quarter of 2026, down from the 36.82% it had in the first quarter of 2025. (Olivia Dean and J. Cole albums were big drivers in the quarter as well.) Each of its competitors — Sony Music Group, Warner Music Group and the collective independent sector — had gains year over year, with Sony closing the gap at the top with a 28.62% share, up more than a percentage point from last year, bringing it to within two points of UMG for the first time in years. (Sony owns indie distributor The Orchard, which has had a massive first three months of 2026.) Warner also jumped, increasing over 2% to 17.98% in the first quarter. But the biggest beneficiary was the indie sector, which grew from 19.92% last year to 22.65% in the first quarter of this year. By label ownership, the indie sector's growth is even more impressive: At 44.15%, indies collectively are by far the largest portion of the current music business in the United States, according to Billboard estimates based on Luminate data. Indies have had a big year so far with both Bad Bunny and Djo being big drivers for their labels, Rimas and Djo Music, respectively. Bad Bunny in particular had an extraordinarily strong beginning of the year, capped by his performance at the Super Bowl halftime show and victory at the Grammys for album of the year. Universal was second at 22.64%, followed by Warner's 17.26% and Sony's 15.95%. Beyond the top three among individual labels, Warner Records came in fourth by current market share with 5.54%, bolstered by a big January release from Zach Bryan, followed closely by a surging Columbia Records, whose 5.49% was up markedly from last year's 4.25%, as Harry Styles, Ella Langley and Dominic Fike began to catch fire. Capitol Music Group (which includes Astralwerks, Blue Note, Capitol Christian and others) stayed in sixth, with a market share gain to 4.92% from last year's 4.56%. RCA dipped to seventh, coming in at 3.23%, dropping from last year's 4.83%, when it placed fifth in the first quarter. Rounding out the top 10 is another trio of Sony labels: Alamo Records (2.11%), Sony Music Nashville (1.91%) and Sony Music Latin (1.74%). Among label groups by distribution ownership, overall market share — which includes catalog titles as well as current — adhered much closer to the standard of the past few years. Universal's industry-leading tally came in at 37.68%, followed by Sony's 28.09% and a 19.38% share for WMG, with the indies at 14.84%. That represents a slight dip for both Universal and the indie sector year over year, while Sony and WMG both grew in market share. Interscope led an extremely close three-way race at the top among individual labels with 9.75%, closely followed by REPUBLIC (9.32%) and Atlantic (9.29%). In catalog share, Universal was even more dominant with a 39.76% share, with Sony at 27.93%, Warner at 19.80% and the indies at 15.35%. Interscope again led a tight race at the top with 10.28%, followed by Atlantic at 9.45% and REPUBLIC at 9.12%. Daily newsletters straight to your inbox Your email address will not be published. Required fields are marked * Post Comment Send us a tip using our anonymous form. A daily briefing on what matters in the music industry Send us a tip using our anonymous form. Arnold Schwarzenegger Comedy 'Man With The Bag' Gets Release Date On Prime Video
Source: Deadline    Published: 2026-05-12 00:22:00

By Justin Kroll Film Editor The holiday family comedy The Man with the Bag, starring Arnold Schwarzenegger and Alan Ritchson, will bow December 2 on Prime Video. The announcement was made at the Prime Video upfronts being held in New York Directed by Adam Shankman and also starring Awkwafina, the film revolves around the theft of Santa's magic bag. After it is stolen, St. Nick (Schwarzenegger) turns to his naughty list to find Vance (Ritchson), a thief, to help him get it back. Along with his daughter, Santa and a group of misfits, Vance will have to pull off the greatest heist of his life to save Christmas. The pic hails from Amazon MGM Studios from a script penned by Allan Rice. Producers for The Man with the Bag include Lawrence Grey, Ben Everard, Reg Tigerman, Dan Spilo and Ritchson. Amazon Upfront: Here's What Happened At Beacon Theater With Oprah, Chris Pratt & Michael B. Jordan
Source: Deadline    Published: 2026-05-12 00:10:00

By Peter White Executive Editor, Television Amazon clearly spared no expense with their Upfront as celebrated DJ and record producer Diplo warmed up the crowd for the streamer's evening event. Kacey Musgraves soon followed with performance of Dry Spell and Butterflies that reminded the crowd that this is no longer your television mother's Upfront. That was reinforced when Oprah Winfrey opened the show after striking a podcast deal with Amazon's Wondery and rapper Ice Spice turned up to promote Twitch and Shaboozey was shilling for Prime Sports. Related Stories News 'Fourth Wing' Gets Amazon Series Order From Outlier Society & Kilter Films; Rebecca Yarros Joins Michael B. Jordan & Lisa Joy For Announcement Cancellations and Renewals 'Jury Duty' Renewed At Amazon For Season 3 In an even more meta moment, Amazon interrupted its own Upfront with actual ads with Summer House star Paige DeSorbo flogging certain items to an increasingly frustrated audience.The company went full in on sports, much like Fox, particularly NFL, bringing NFL MVP and LA Rams QB Matthew Stafford on stage alongside its Thursday Night Football hosts Charissa Thompson, Andrew Whitworth and Ryan Fitzpatrick. It was nearly an hour in before Amazon talked scripted television, bringing out the cast of Off Campus, which was renewed for a second season ahead of its premiere. Watch on Deadline Then came Global Head of TV Peter Friedlander and Head of Film Courtenay Valenti. Friedlander noted the success of shows such as The Summer I Turned Pretty, which he called a “cultural phenomenon” that is now being turned into a movie. “One of the things we do better than anyone in entertainment is reaching young adult audiences. These are the viewers shaping culture in real time, driving conversation, building fandoms, and bringing brands along for the ride,” he added. The former Neflix exec highlighted its books-to-screen pipeline. “Many of those stories did start on screen, they started on the page, and that's the Amazon advantage. Through our store, Audible and Kindle, we have a direct line to what fans are reading and loving. When we adapt these stories for Prime Video, the engagement is immediate,” he added. Chris Pratt turned up to reveal that The Terminal List is finally returning to the service in October, over four years after season one premiered. Friedlander then plugged The Boys spinoff Vought Rising, Reacher spinoff Neagley, Charlie Hunnam-led Criminal and its Carrie adaptation. Meghann Fahy then joined Jane Krakowski in pulling double duty, having previously been at the NBCUniversal Upfront, but was promoting feature film You Deserve Each Other, part of Valenti's latest slate. Michael B. Jordan then closed things out with three major projects: Muhammad Ali drama series The Greatest, starring Jaalen Best as the boxer, Fourth Wing and Creed series spinoff Delphi. “As you all know, we live in some challenging times, and I think a lot about what I'm going to leave behind and the impact that I have on the people around me. One man comes to mind when I think of the impact and work. I'm so thankful that I get to be a small part of telling the story of legendary Muhammad Ali. No athlete, no celebrity, no public figure, had an impact quite like Ali,” he said. It was déjà vu as Amazon closed out its Upfront with Arnold Schwarzenegger. Last year, The Terminator star was in New York to shill for The Man With The Bag but had to be pulled off stage by his True Lies colleague Jamie Lee Curtis. He was back this year to promote the same film, which was a good analogy for Upfront week. Get our Breaking News Alerts and Keep your inbox happy. Comments On Deadline Hollywood are monitored. So don't go off topic, don't impersonate anyone, and don't get your facts wrong. OTHER

Hantavirus Outbreak Is Reviving Covid-19 Conspiracy Theories Online
Source: WIRED Middle East    Published: 2026-05-12 09:34:11

Within hours of reports emerging about a hantavirus outbreak aboard the Dutch-flagged luxury cruise ship MV Hondius, the internet had already decided what was happening. Across X, Bluesky and Reddit's wildly popular r/conspiracy thread, users revived the same depopulation and bioweapon theories that dominated conspiracy corners during the Covid-19 pandemic. The hashtag #Hantavirus quickly became attached to posts from many of the same accounts that pushed “Scamdemic” narratives and antivaccine rhetoric in 2020 and beyond. The ship had been travelling through South America when passengers began falling ill with the Andes strain of hantavirus, a rare rodent-borne disease and one of the few hantavirus variants known to spread between humans in limited circumstances. On X, many users claimed the outbreak would usher in another manufactured global lockdown. “This is not Covid, this is not influenza. It spreads very, very differently,” Maria Van Kerkhove, director of epidemic and pandemic management at the World Health Organization, said in response to the growing comparisons. Anti-WHO rhetoric – most commonly associated with right-wing profiles such as @MAGAVoice — quickly revived the “plandemic” narrative, a conspiracy canon popularised by the widely discredited 2020 pseudo-documentary of the same name. “They are going to try and do it again… Buckle up…” the MAGAVoice account posted to its 1.3 million followers. Trump pulled out of the WHO soon after taking office and has so far remained mostly absent from the global hantavirus response. Other posts pointed to past reporting around potential hantavirus vaccines and resurfaced old comments from billionaire Bill Gates warning that future pandemics could emerge after Covid. On X, far-right conspiracy theorist Alex Jones declared “Globalists Launch Covid 2.0 As Hantavirus Spreads Worldwide” to his 4.5 million followers. Elsewhere, antivaccine figures such as former US representative Marjorie Taylor Greene amplified Ivermectin claims from Texas otolaryngologist Mary Talley Bowden – claims that have since been fact-checked for spreading misinformation – while accusing pharmaceutical companies of manufacturing outbreaks for profit and pushing unsupported claims about zinc or vitamin D neutralising the virus. Other accounts warned followers not to take a hantavirus vaccine, despite none yet existing. Some described the outbreak as a bioweapon – a biological agent such as a virus, bacteria, fungus or toxin deliberately used to cause harm, disease or death. Prominent right-wing accounts resurrected familiar “depopulation” narratives suggesting pharmaceutical companies were engineering another health crisis for profit. A 2022 tweet reading simply, “2023: Corona ended. 2026: Hantavirus,” exploded across social media after the outbreak, with conspiracy commenters flooding the post as supposed proof that the outbreak had been planned years in advance. Experts at the WHO continue to stress that “this is not another Covid.” Unlike SARS‑CoV‑2, hantavirus is not a novel virus. It has existed for decades, and while the Andes strain involved in the outbreak can spread between humans in rare cases, person-to-person transmission remains extremely limited. The rhetoric surrounding hantavirus feels almost eerily recycled from 2020. Claims that the outbreak is being orchestrated to justify lockdowns, expand surveillance or influence elections have re-emerged in near-identical form to the narratives that circulated during Covid. One viral post suggested the outbreak would coincide with the 2026 Fifa World Cup and US elections to justify tighter controls and expanded mail-in voting systems, a theory that appeared to gain traction among some Trump supporters. Others claimed QR-code tracking systems would return under the guise of public health measures. Elsewhere, conspiracies involving Jeffrey Epstein suggest evidence from the recent files point to him as the game maker behind the “plandemic”. At the same time, conspiracy accounts pointed suspiciously towards pharmaceutical companies already researching potential hantavirus vaccines. On Instagram, many users claimed Moderna was “already working on a vaccine before the outbreak even started”. Other antivax creators speculated that the 2025 deaths of actor Gene Hackman and his wife were linked to hantavirus, largely based on misguided reporting around the case. Meanwhile, creators resurrected clips from The Simpsons as supposed evidence that entertainment media had “predicted” the outbreak years in advance, echoing the same style of “predictive programming” theories that spread widely during the coronavirus pandemic. In an episode titled “Hello Gutter, Hello Fadder”, Bart Simpson, his mother Marge and sister Lisa watch a television segment in which a TV personality jokes about catching hantavirus. Users also circulated clips from “A Totally Fun Thing Bart Will Never Do Again”, a cruise-ship-themed episode in which passengers become trapped aboard a vessel after an outbreak spreads among guests. “The Simpsons Warned Us!” one X user wrote while sharing clips from the episode. “Hantavirus came from a boat in 2012 … Coincidence? I don't think so!” Conspiracy communities also drew parallels to the 1998 film The X-Files: Fight the Future, in which a staged hantavirus outbreak is used as a cover story to conceal an alien invasion. Users linked the storyline to recent political discussions around extraterrestrial life and secret breeding programmes. As the outbreak continues to dominate online discourse, many of the conspiracy theories surrounding it continue to spread without evidence. But the speed at which old pandemic narratives have resurfaced suggests that the internet's Covid-era conspiracy infrastructure never really disappeared – it simply found a new outbreak to attach itself to. More From WIRED More from Condé Nast Middle East © 2026 Condé Nast. All rights reserved. A Christian View of UFO's, Aliens, and Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena
Source: breakpoint.org    Published: 2026-05-12 08:07:28

In 1976, Carl F.H. Henry published God, Revelation, and Authority, one of the most significant works of theology in the twentieth century. Five years later, Gordon Clark released A Christian View of Men and Things. In the same tradition, David Noebel's magisterial Understanding the Times covered ten different areas to which a worldview speaks, from theology and philosophy to economics, ethics, sociology, and history. And all on his own, the late Christian philosopher Ron Nash covered almost every other conceivable topic from a Christian worldview. Missing in the long line of Christian worldview books is one that would have been especially helpful last week when the Pentagon released a slew of documents, some of which were formerly classified. The Christian view of UFO's, Aliens, and Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena (or UAP) remains to be written. NPR described this move by the Trump administration, “In all, the Pentagon released more than 160 records, citing President Trump's call for unprecedented transparency in giving the public access to federal and military records related to unexplained encounters with strange phenomena.” The released files date back as far as the 1940's and include reports of the unexplained from military sources, government officials, and even the crew of Apollo 11. So far, the response has ranged from a buzz by enthusiasts to a sense by others of another let down by a government over-promising but, in the end, delivering a nothing-burger. Also, in a strange pre-release twist, a group of Pentecostal pastors claimed to have been briefed by U.S. Military intelligence officers, who warned of an imminent “crisis of faith” due to the released documents. Thankfully, at least so far, no such crisis has materialized. When it comes to aliens and life on other planets, the Bible is basically silent. In both the Narnia series and his Space Trilogy, C.S. Lewis seemed to entertain the idea of other worlds populated by non-human creatures created and loved by God. There is no biblical support for such worlds. Though the Bible does not explicitly reject the possibility, neither does It even imply it is a possibility. The only biblical precedent that is applicable was acknowledged by Lewis and applied to his imagined worlds: namely, the universal reign of Christ over all that is created in heaven and earth. In all worlds, if there are any other worlds, Christ is over and above every created power. The Bible does discuss and, at times, describes creatures that are neither human nor animal. These beings exist, not on other planets but in a different realm, unseen but with access to our own. They are not human, nor do they bear the image of God. Recently, in a conversation about artificial intelligence—another topic that is captivating hearts and minds with both hope and fear—the brilliant apologist and Oxford mathematician John Lennox reminded me that, in Scripture, conversations about the unknown are joined by the admonition, “Be not deceived.” Paul warned that, in the future, even the elect would be at risk for deception except for the goodness of God in securing our hearts and minds. In other words, if a release of secret documents or any future revelation about unexplained phenomena causes a “crisis of faith” among believers, that would say more about the state of the Church than about aliens. According to the Biblical record, among the creatures that exist are both the angelic and the demonic, and neither are made in the image of God. In other words, when thinking about any other living creatures in the universe, human exceptionalism is a starting point for discernment. The Genesis account provides a plain description of the cosmos and of who we are. God exists. He made everything that is. Humans are made in His image. Angels are curious about this, and demons are jealous. Another phrase repeated in Holy Scripture about the unknown, often said by angelic beings visiting humans, is “fear not.” As John Donne wrote, “. . . he that fears God, fears nothing else.” Thus, it is possible to be fearless about the unknown, even that which seems able to overwhelm our strength and our senses. In a couple of books, the late historian Rodney Stark observed that early Christians condemned a fear of supernatural beings, believing that Satan's defeat by Christ was definitive and that the Resurrection had enthroned Him above all other powers. That is, of course, what Scripture proclaims. In other words, when thinking about things like UFO's, aliens, and UAP's, the best place to start is with the rule and reign of Christ over all things. With the certainty that the Christ who has risen is eternally triumphant over all that is seen and unseen, we can properly understand even the unexplainable. Christian HistoryPolitics & GovernmentScience/Nature/Creation/Technology "I wanted to.... teach my congregation to live in the world faithfully and not just share a gospel presentation. It's about being able to tell a story about what God's doing in the world." © Copyright 2020, All Rights Reserved. The NikeSKIMS Rift Mesh "Psychic Pink" Is the Collab's Boldest Drop Yet
Source: Hypebeast    Published: 2026-05-12 07:29:01

Sign up for a Hype account, a new unified account accessing unique features and benefits across all Hypebeast websites. Some languages may be automatically translated. Accuracy may vary. Get the HYPE App Stay ahead of the curve and never miss out on what's fresh, exciting, and essential. Scan the QR code above to download the HYPE app now, or check it out in the app stores. Trending Name: NikeSKIMS Rift Mesh “Psychic Pink” Colorway: Psychic Pink/Psychic Pink/Psychic Pink SKU: IO7694-600 MSRP: $150 USD Release Date: May 14 Where to Buy: Nike Kim Kardashian's SKIMS and Nike's collaborative NikeSKIMS Rift Mesh is back with a new colorway of the collaborative split-toe silhouette. “Psychic Pink” arrives May 14 in a head-to-toe bubble gum pink finish, marking the collab's most visually assertive release to date. The construction stays consistent with the Rift Mesh's established build. Mesh covers the upper, keeping the silhouette lightweight and breathable through the warmer months. Soft foam underfoot maintains the shoe's lounge-ready comfort positioning, a through-line across every NikeSKIMS Rift Mesh release since the partnership's January debut. The hook-and-loop strap at the midfoot provides an adjustable fit without compromising the low-profile silhouette, and the outsole carries SKIMS “S” logo detailing in place of standard rubber tread, one of the more quietly distinctive construction choices in the collab's toolkit. What “Psychic Pink” changes is the color register. The NikeSKIMS Rift Mesh launched in January 2026 across three colorways: Black, Velvet Brown, and Archaeo Brown, all grounded in the earth-tone minimalism that has defined SKIMS' clothing aesthetic since the brand's inception. A Satin iteration followed in February, again operating within a muted palette. “Psychic Pink” represents the first time the partnership has committed to a loud, singular color across the entire shoe, with the same shade running from the split-toe cap through the mesh upper, midfoot strap, and co-branded heel with no tonal variation or contrast detailing to break it up. The monochrome execution is consistent with the silhouette's design logic, as the Rift Mesh has always treated the whole shoe as a single surface, but the color choice marks a deliberate pivot toward summer and a broader consumer than the one the earth-tone launches were speaking to. The timing aligns with a wider industry movement toward expressive color in women's footwear, and within the NikeSKIMS line specifically, the “Psychic Pink” drop signals that the partnership is willing to test the limits of its own established palette. The NikeSKIMS Rift Mesh launched in January 2026 across three colorways: Black, Velvet Brown, and Archaeo Brown, all grounded in the earth-tone minimalism that has defined SKIMS' clothing aesthetic since the brand's inception. A Satin iteration followed in February, again operating within a muted palette. “Psychic Pink” represents the first time the partnership has committed to a loud, singular color across the entire shoe, with the same shade running from the split-toe cap through the mesh upper, midfoot strap, and co-branded heel with no tonal variation or contrast detailing to break it up. The monochrome execution is consistent with the silhouette's design logic, as the Rift Mesh has always treated the whole shoe as a single surface, but the color choice marks a deliberate pivot toward summer and a broader consumer than the one the earth-tone launches were speaking to. New batch of UFO records from feds reveals secret meetings at Los Alamos
Source: Santa Fe New Mexican    Published: 2026-05-12 04:45:00

Advertisement Reporter Real consternation over “green fireball” phenomena and other shadowy nighttime sightings prompted astrophysicists and FBI officials to assemble for a conference in Los Alamos in 1949, questing for explanations. The meetings — at least one attended by famed physicist Edward Teller, the “father of the hydrogen bomb” — came after Los Alamos National Laboratory employees and military pilots reported strange aerial phenomena that seemed to be abounding near government and military installations, according to a 1950 report from a U.S. Air Force district commander. All of this led the Air Force sometime around 1950 to pursue “Project Twinkle,” which set up 24-hour outposts to observe and photograph “unidentified aerial phenomena” in New Mexico, including in the windswept desert town of Vaughn in Guadalupe County. It is no secret now the U.S. government secretly studied “unidentified flying objects” for years, but newly released, once-confidential documents shed even more light on the federal government's investigation into sightings in the Land of Enchantment, particularly near military bases and national laboratories. Unsurprisingly, New Mexico is featured at length in the new cache of UFO records released by the Pentagon last week. The state has been a reliable hotspot on contemporary UFO reporting platforms and has long been synonymous with UFO lore due to the publicity of the “Roswell Incident,” among other such incidents. “Since 1948, approximately 150 observations of aerial phenomena referred to above have been recorded in the vicinity of installations in New Mexico,” wrote FBI Agent A.H. Belmont in a memorandum dated August 1950 that was made public last week. The thousands of pages of files, many of which report sightings of strange things in the night sky, are available through a new government website, war.gov/UFO. A message on the website indicates more records will be forthcoming while touting President Donald Trump's commitment to “transparency.” “The materials archived here are unresolved cases, meaning the government is unable to make a definitive determination on the nature of the observed phenomena,” the message states. “This can occur for a variety of reasons, including a lack of sufficient data, and the Department of War welcomes the application of private-sector analysis, information and expertise.” UFO researchers have theories about the large volume of sightings in New Mexico, citing how alien mythology and lore have been woven into the state's cultural fabric for decades. According to the National UFO Reporting Center, 1,800 sightings have been reported in New Mexico, most of them since 2000. Obsession with unidentified flying objects continues nationwide, even after a congressionally mandated Pentagon report released in February found no evidence the federal government was covering up knowledge of extraterrestrial technology and no evidence UFO sightings are signs of aliens visiting Earth. Investigations by the FBI into “flying discs,” “flying saucers,” and “balls of fire” in Northern New Mexico's skies, specifically in the vicinity of Los Alamos National Laboratory, were considered “top secret” by the agency in 1949. In a memo addressed to the FBI director from the special FBI agent in charge, pages detail that “unexplained phenomena” had been observed near the Atomic Energy Commission installation at the national laboratory on at least nine dates in December 1948 — a period when unusual objects in the sky were spotted on successive days above the Pajarito Plateau area. Infrared image from the FBI files of an unidentified flying object over a mountain range in the western U.S. in 2025. The photo is a part of a trove of documentation on UFOs released by the federal government last week. “Some nine scientific reasons are stated to exist which indicated that the phenomenon observed are not due to meteorites,” the memo said. “The only conclusions reached thus far are that they are either hitherto unobserved natural phenomena or that they are man made. No scientific experiments are known to exist in this country which could give rise to such phenomena.” But another memo to the FBI director dated 1949 indicated federal agents believed the U.S. government's own top-secret research may have been the source of all of the apparent panic. “There appears to be reason to believe that the above-mentioned phenomenon may be connected with secret experiments being conducted by some U.S. Government Agency as it is believed that the United States is further advanced in guided missile development than any foreign power,” one memo to the FBI director in the files reads. On its website, Los Alamos National Laboratory says the notion green fireballs were a national security threat was “ultimately dismissed,” the official conclusion being that they were likely “natural in origin.” The Roswell incident is naturally the best known, drawing believers from around the U.S. to an annual UFO festival in a city that has situated itself uniquely at the heart of UFO tourism. In 1947, the U.S. Army Air Force sent out a news release announcing it had found the remnants of a flying saucer at a crash site out in the desert. But the military quickly retracted that statement, saying it was really a weather balloon. In the 1990s, the Air Force said the debris was actually from Project Mogul, a top-secret project to attempt to detect Soviet Union atomic bomb tests with high-altitude balloons that had been tested out of Alamogordo Army Air Field. The Socorro incident in April 1964, on the other hand — unresolved in the Air Force investigations that transpired at the time and widely reported by New Mexico news publications — was observed by local police Officer Lonnie Zamora. He claimed he witnessed two humanoids beside a shiny, egg-shaped object that later rose into the air from an arroyo as flames belched out. The dramatic and vivid case did not go unnoticed by the federal government. The FBI report from the 1964 incident also was released Friday, tucked away in the sprawling trove of digitized paperwork. It begins by characterizing Zamora as a “sober, dependable, mature, not of fancy, officer of police department Socorro, N.M.” Seeing a flame in an isolated area about a mile south of Socorro, Zamora observed “two persons in apparent coveralls adjacent to the object,” which initially looked to the officer like an overturned car but which he later described, upon drawing closer to the scene, as oval-shaped and about 20 feet long with red insignia, according to the FBI report. The report says Zamora described seeing the “flying object rise from the earth with a roar, emitting bluish and orange flames and disappear over a distant mountain.” Local law enforcement officials who later arrived to the scene observed “four small irregularly shaped smoldering areas.” Zamora alone witnessed the alleged event. He died in 2009. Over the years, various explanations have been posed, including a test of some sort at White Sands Missile Range or a hoax performed by students at what is now known as New Mexico Institute of Mining and Technology. New Mexico newspapers reported on other alleged UFO landings around the time of the Socorro Incident, including in Luis Lopez, Polvadera and in the Rio Arriba County community of La Madera. New Mexico is featured at length in the new cache of UFO records released by the Pentagon last week. The state reliably has been a hotspot on contemporary UFO reporting platforms online and has long been synonymous with UFO lore due to the publicity of the “Roswell Incident.” Real consternation over the “green fireball” phenomena and other shadowy nighttime sightings prompted astrophysicists and Federal Bureau of Investigation officials to assemble in 1949 for a conference in Los Alamos, searching for explanations. Thousands of pages of reports released by the Pentagon last week show how the U.S. government secretly studied UFOs for years, including in New Mexico. The company, founded in 2020, has issued a new report naming New Mexico its top state for sightings per capita. Podcaster claims government still hides answers after UFO document release
Source: KUTV    Published: 2026-05-12 02:22:52

Now 67° Tue 94° Wed 90° by Avery Sloane, Mary Nickles & Jared Turner, KUTV SALT LAKE CITY (KUTV) — The Pentagon has released hundreds of never-before-seen files tied to unidentified flying objects, giving the public a new look at decades of government investigations into unexplained sightings. More documents are expected to be released in the coming weeks. Those who have wondered what the government knows about UFOs can now look for themselves. UFO researcher and podcaster Jim Quirk spoke with ARC Salt Lake about the newly released files and why he believes the government is still withholding key information about unidentified aerial phenomena. The Pentagon has released hundreds of never-before-seen files tied to unidentified flying objects, giving the public a new look at decades of government investigations into unexplained sightings. More documents are expected to be released in the coming weeks. (KUTV) Quirk said he has not had the chance to go through everything the government released. “There's a lot of information there. It's hard to go through absolutely everything, but I got a good taste of it by what I did see,” he said. “It's a lot of videos, it's a lot of documents.” Quirk said many of the files appear to show unusual objects captured on military cameras, though he believes the government has still stopped short of fully explaining what they are. “It's exciting for people who are interested in this subject. At the same time, this does not represent disclosure of any sort,” Quirk said. “We're still not there yet. We're not at the disclosure point. The government is still covering things up.” Quirk believes some of the information being withheld could involve recovered aircraft or technology that officials do not want made public. “They most certainly have hidden from the public recovered alien craft, or non-human intelligent craft. Some people believe that these beings are from here, somehow,” he said. Quirk claimed the government has been hiding the truth of alien aircraft for 80 years, and that eventually they would release additional information. “I think they're starting to come clean because they want to keep this in the public view. They want the public to think about this for a while, and then they're going to release all the information at some point in the future,” he said. Still, Quirk said he doubts the government will ever fully reveal everything it knows about UFOs or unexplained aerial sightings. “I really find it difficult to believe the government would come out with full disclosure, because maybe there are some things that they would be afraid to share with the public,” Quirk said. “Who knows what the reaction would be.” When asked which of the newly released materials stood out the most, Quirk pointed to one unusual video in particular. “There was a star-shaped object that was moving across the sky, and was filmed in the Middle East somewhere just a few years ago. What is it – I don't know. It's very exciting,” he said. Quirk said some people believed the object was either a camera anomaly or just a flare. “See, the problem is the government releases this stuff, but at the same time, they don't provide a full explanation, and they say they don't know what it is,” he said. “I believe some people in the government do know what it is, and they're not being fully transparent about that.” _____ 2026 Sinclair, Inc.

Joe Rogan chooses a side in Trump assassination attempt setup debate
Source: wonderwall.com    Published: 2026-05-12 00:58:50

Podcaster Joe Rogan has rejected conspiracy theories claiming the July 2024 assassination attempt on President Donald Trump was staged, saying that anyone with knowledge of firearms would know that it was not a hoax. Speaking on a recent episode of his “The Joe Rogan Experience” podcast, Rogan argued the shooting in Butler, Pa., could not realistically have been orchestrated as a political stunt. During the podcast conversation, Rogan forcefully pushed back on claims that the 2024 shooting was staged to generate sympathy for Trump ahead of the election. “There's a lot of people running around out there saying that that first Trump assassination [attempt] was a setup and it was a hoax and that Trump did it to get people to be more sympathetic to him,” Rogan said. “Anybody who says that doesn't know anything about guns.” Rogan focused on the technical difficulty of the shot fired during the Butler rally. “There is not a person on earth that could nick your ear at 140 yards reliably,” he said. “And just by the grace of God, by just sheer luck, he turns his head at the right time, and he catches his ear?” He also pointed out that the shooter missed other shots first and that the gunfire killed one attendee and wounded another, arguing that those facts further undermine claims that the attack was coordinated. The assassination attempt occurred on July 13, 2024, during a Trump campaign rally in Butler. Authorities identified the shooter as Thomas Matthew Crooks, who fired multiple rounds toward the stage from an elevated position outside the rally perimeter, wounding Trump's ear. Rally attendee Corey Comperatore was killed and two others were seriously injured before a Secret Service counter-sniper fatally shot Crooks. The incident triggered bipartisan concern over escalating political violence and raised scrutiny over security failures that allowed the gunman to gain a clear line of sight toward the stage. On his podcast, Rogan described the episode as a “tremendous failure of security.” Rogan's comments come a month after former Congresswoman Marjorie Taylor Greene, a noted Trump ally-turned-critic, wrote on X that while she is “not calling the Butler assassination [attempt] a hoax,” she still has questions about it. “There are a lot of questions that deserve public answers. I'm asking why won't Trump release the information about Matthew Crooks? Did he actually act alone? If not, who is behind him and who helped him? Why the cover up??” she wrote on social media on April 19. She further wrote that Comperatore's “wife and daughters deserve to know why Corey, a true American patriot and hero was murdered in Butler.” Her posts sparked backlash from close Trump ally and far-right activist Laura Loomer, who accused Greene and others of promoting “the deranged conspiracy theory that President Trump staged his attempted assassination in Butler, Pennsylvania.” The attempted assassination of Trump in July 2024 became one of the defining moments of that election cycle and intensified concerns about the security threats facing public officials. The discussion surrounding the Butler shooting has intensified amid several high-profile violent incidents over the past year, including the assassinations of conservative activist Charlie Kirk and Democratic lawmaker Melissa Hortman. Concerns grew again after the April 25 shooting at the White House Correspondents' Association dinner, which authorities say was another assassination attempt targeting Trump. Prosecutors charged Cole Tomas Allen, a California teacher, with attempting to assassinate the president after investigators said he breached security and opened fire near the event venue.
